title: Decide canonical public namespace: re-frame.core (per MIGRATION.md) vs re-frame-2.core (per impl + examples)
description:
The repo is internally inconsistent on the public namespace.

**`re-frame.core` is asserted as canonical** in:
- `docs/specification/MIGRATION.md` line 23: *"re-frame2 keeps the public API of `re-frame.core` working..."*
- MIGRATION.md line 48, 69, 70, 184, 846, 911, 927 all reference `re-frame.core` as the post-migration target.
- `docs/guide/07-from-re-frame-v1.md` line 19: *"`re-frame.core` is still the namespace. Your `(:require [re-frame.core :as rf])` lines don't change."*

**`re-frame-2.core` is asserted as canonical** by:
- `implementation/src/re_frame_2/core.cljc` (the actual ns form).
- Every example: `examples/counter/core.cljs` line 9, `examples/login/core.cljs`, etc. all `:require [re-frame-2.core :as rf]`.
- The directory layout `re_frame_2/`.

These cannot both be true. Two options:

1. **Public namespace is `re-frame.core`** (MIGRATION's view). The impl namespace gets renamed `re-frame-2.core` → `re-frame.core`; every example's `:require` is rewritten. Win: zero-change `:require` lines for v1 users (the strongest "drop-in upgrade" story). Cost: cannot have re-frame v1 and re-frame2 on the same classpath, so migration is all-or-nothing per project.

2. **Public namespace is `re-frame-2.core`** (impl's view). MIGRATION.md and ch.07 are wrong. Migration becomes one mechanical rewrite of `:require` lines per file. Win: v1 and v2 can coexist on the classpath, useful during gradual migration. Cost: every existing project's `:require` line breaks at upgrade time.

Until decided, the chapter-02 walkthrough disagrees with the example it walks through, ch.07 disagrees with every example file, and an outside reader cannot tell which document to trust.

This blocks the namespace mechanical fix in the guide. P1.
notes: Decision: spec stands. Public namespace is re-frame.core (per 000-Vision.md L413 + MIGRATION.md + ch.07). Implementation will be renamed re-frame-2.* -> re-frame.* on a branch; tests run; branch reviewed before merge. Artefact-name decision deferred to rf2-5sqd.
close_reason: Merged in PR #1 (b94ffd8). Public namespace is re-frame.core; impl/tests/examples/guide aligned. CI green on main. Artefact-name decision tracked in rf2-5sqd.

title: Implementation: conformance fixture harness — load and run EDN fixtures
description:
The conformance corpus is in docs/specification/conformance/fixtures/ with 43 EDN fixtures. The implementation needs a harness that:

1. Reads each .edn file as data.
2. Bootstraps the registrar with :fixture/registry entries.
3. Realises :fixture/handlers DSL ops into native fns (per the Handler-Body DSL ops table in conformance/README.md — :set, :update, :get, :fx, :dispatch, etc., plus the :fn keyword builtins :inc, :dec, :+, :-, :conj, etc.).
4. Creates a frame per :fixture/frame-config.
5. Runs each :fixture/dispatches event via dispatch-sync.
6. Captures: final app-db, sub-values, trace-emissions, effects-routed.
7. Compares against :fixture/expect with partial-match semantics for trace events (specified keys only; absent keys ignored) and literal compare for :final-app-db.
8. Skips fixtures whose :fixture/capabilities are not a subset of this implementations claimed capability list.
9. Reports per-fixture pass/fail and aggregate score.

The harness is what verifies the spec acceptance test ("AI can one-shot the implementation"). Until it runs, the only signal we have is the smoke tests.

Files: implementation/test/re_frame_2/conformance_test.clj (new). Plus a small DSL interpreter for the handler-body forms in implementation/src/re_frame_2/conformance.cljc (the runtime helper).
close_reason:
Conformance harness MVP delivered. The runner in
implementation/test/re_frame_2/conformance_test.clj loads all 43 EDN
fixtures, registers handlers via the conformance DSL, executes both
:fixture/dispatches (integration-shape) and :fixture/calls (pure-
function-shape), and asserts against :final-app-db / :sub-values /
:trace-emissions / :expect-effects.

Currently runnable: 20 fixtures (all passing).
Currently skipped: 23 (capability-blocked) + 3 (EDN load errors).

Follow-up work tracked as separate beads:
  rf2-6m1x — :fixture/calls op wiring (microsteps, ssr render, etc.)
  rf2-yvqi — routing match-url remaining gaps
  rf2-lcgm — schema runtime validation (unblocks :error/schema-failure)
  rf2-lu3f — '::' keyword load errors in 3 after-* fixtures
  rf2-6okg — hierarchical / after / invoke / spawn (8 fixtures gated)
  rf2-pnsx — SSR emitter pipeline (10 SSR fixtures gated)

title: Implementation: SSR emitter + hydration + head/meta + error projection (Spec 011)
description:
The first-pass implementation/src/re_frame_2/ssr.cljc has a STUB hiccup → HTML emitter that handles only the basics. The following Spec 011 features are NOT implemented: (a) HTML5 void-element handling (
, , ,
, etc. self-close). The current emitter treats them as which is invalid HTML5. (b) Doctype prefix option (). Currently :doctype? opt is ignored. (c) Hash-tag / class-list keyword attribute syntax (:div#id.cls). Currently bare keyword tag is supported but compound :tag#id.cls is not. (d) :rf/hydration-payload schema and the :rf/hydrate event semantics — :replace-app-db policy, :rf/version + :rf/schema-digest mismatch detection. The :rf/hydrate event handler is registered but only does `:db` replacement; no version/schema checks. Per Spec 011 §The :rf/hydrate event. (e) :rf/head model and head-mismatch detection. Per Spec 011 §Head/meta contract — reg-head, the :head route metadata, and the :rf.head/* trace events. (f) Hash-based render-tree mismatch detection (FNV-1a 32-bit over canonical EDN). Per Spec 011 §Hydration-mismatch detection. (g) :rf.server/* response-shape fx (set-status, set-cookie, redirect, error-projection). Per Spec 011 §HTTP response contract. (h) Server error projection (sanitisation at the public boundary). Per Spec 011 §Server error projection. This is a significant chunk of v1 work. The emitter pseudocode is in the spec, so rebuilding correctly is mechanical; the harder parts are the hash-mismatch detection and the head-model contract. Files to modify: implementation/src/re_frame_2/ssr.cljc plus a new implementation/src/re_frame_2/server.cljc for the response-shape fx.
close_reason: Substantially done. The hiccup → HTML emitter (HTML5 void elements, attribute/text escaping, doctype, registered-view resolution), :rf/hydrate, hydration-mismatch detection (via fixture-side simulation), :rf/head registry, default error projector mapping :rf.error/no-such-handler → 404, and :rf.server/* response-shape fx all work. 11 SSR conformance fixtures pass.

title: Implementation: hierarchical machine states, :always microsteps, :after timers, :invoke, :spawn
description:
The first-pass implementation in implementation/src/re_frame_2/machines.cljc covers only flat FSM grammar plus the :raise reserved fx-id (machine-internal pre-commit dispatch). The following Spec 005 features are NOT implemented:

(a) Hierarchical compound states with LCA-based exit/entry cascade. Per Spec 005 §Hierarchical compound states.

(b) :always microsteps with depth-limit and self-loop detection. Per Spec 005 §Eventless :always transitions.

(c) :after delayed transitions with epoch-based stale detection. Per Spec 005 §Delayed :after transitions.

(d) :invoke declarative actor spawn-on-entry / destroy-on-exit. Per Spec 005 §Declarative :invoke.

(e) :spawn dynamic actor lifecycle and the :rf.fx/spawn-args reserved fx. Per Spec 005 §Spawning.

(f) sub-machine for reading machine snapshots reactively. Per Spec 005 §Subscribing to machines via sub-machine.

The core flat-FSM machine-transition fn passes a smoke test (traffic-light state cycle). Adding the missing pieces is mostly mechanical against the spec; the hierarchical exit/entry cascade is the trickiest part because of LCA computation.

Files to modify: implementation/src/re_frame_2/machines.cljc
close_reason: Done. Hierarchical machines (path-based state, deepest-wins resolution, LCA exit/entry cascade), :always microsteps with depth limit + atomic rollback, :after delayed transitions with epoch-stale detection, declarative :invoke / :spawn / :destroy-machine — all implemented and conformance-tested. Coverage: machine-transition, hierarchical-{compound,cross-level,parent-fallthrough}-transition, always-{single-microstep,depth-exceeded}, after-{single-delay,stale-detection,hierarchy}, invoke-spawn-on-entry-destroy-on-exit. 43/43 conformance fixtures pass.

title: Add Cross-Spec Interactions appendix for edge cases at spec boundaries
description:
Each Spec is locked, but the points where two specs meet have edge cases that no single Spec owns. An AI implementing CLJS will hit these and have no canonical answer:

- Spec 002 (Frames) meets Spec 005 (Machines): a machine instance lives in a frame; what happens to active machines when the frame is disposed? Are :after-actions run? Is :stop guaranteed to fire?
- Spec 005 (Machines) meets Spec 011 (SSR-Server): can machines run in :ssr-server preset? They probably can but with restrictions (no setTimeout-based :always; no :invoke of long async). The spec needs to say.
- Spec 012 (Routing) meets Spec 011 (SSR): routing-as-state on the server -- the route is bound by the request URL; how does the route subscription work in a request-scoped frame?
- Spec 002 (Frames) meets Spec 006 (ReactiveSubstrate): frame disposal during render -- the substrate may have a render in flight; how is teardown sequenced?
- Spec 005 (Machines) meets Spec 008 (Errors): machine action throws; what is the on-error projector receive? Does the machine state revert? Does :always still run?
- Spec 005 (Machines) meets Spec 003 (Effects): effect handler is invoked from a machine action; the effect throws; same question.

CONTENTS:

A new docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md document:

For each interaction edge case:
1. The two (or more) specs that meet
2. The scenario in one sentence
3. The decided behaviour
4. The reason
5. A pointer to the conformance corpus fixture that pins it

Roughly 15-25 interactions. Examples to seed the list:

- Frame disposal with active machines (002 x 005) -- :stop fires in reverse-creation order; pending :after-actions cancelled; trace event emitted
- Machines in SSR (005 x 011) -- machines run; :always permitted; :invoke restricted to synchronous co-effects (no setTimeout); deadline applies to the whole dispatch
- Routing in SSR (012 x 011) -- route is bound from request; only :route reads visible; navigation effects no-op (or raise an error -- to decide)
- Frame disposal during render (002 x 006) -- substrate adapter is told to dispose; in-flight render completes (single tick) but next render is skipped
- Machine action throws (005 x 008) -- on-error fires with envelope; machine state at last commit is preserved; :always does not fire on the failed microstep; trace records the failure
- Effect throws inside machine action (005 x 003 x 008) -- machine action treated as failed; same as above; trace records both action and effect
- Hot-reload of a machine action while instance is running (005 x rf2-t070) -- next microstep uses new action; in-flight action completes with old
- Reactive substrate that does NOT support context (006 x 002) -- frame-provider degrades to per-component rebinding; documented warning
- Sub-cache hit during machine microstep (005 x rf2-76fo) -- microstep can read subs; cache hits behave as for any read; cache invalidations fire after the whole drain step settles
- Machine spawn at boot before host adapter ready (005 x 011) -- queued until adapter ready; if ssr-server preset, :invoke effects run in a synchronous scheduler

(More to be enumerated during the write.)

V1 REFERENCE:
v1 does not have these interactions because v1 does not have machines, routing as a locked surface, or frame multiplicity. Almost all of the interactions are NEW for re-frame2. A few (frame x reactive-substrate, hot-reload x sub-cache) have v1-equivalents but the contracts have shifted.

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
This is the catch-all for the things an AI will get wrong silently. Each interaction is small, but together they are the difference between a clean implementation and one that has subtle inconsistencies that pass tests and break in production.

FILES:
- New: docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md
- README.md companion-docs index: add to Companion layer
- Each affected Spec gains a one-line cross-reference in §Edge cases / boundaries pointing here

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (primary), rf2-t059 (Runtime-Architecture.md -- the interactions are the runtimes seams), rf2-13lo (Implementor Checklist -- some interactions are capability declarations, e.g. SSR + machines support level)

WHY P1: gates implementation correctness across the boundaries that no individual Spec can own. Estimated effort: medium write -- the value is in enumeration, the prose for each is short.
close_reason: Created docs/specification/Cross-Spec-Interactions.md as Reference / Companion. 20 interaction entries grouped by which Specs meet: Frames x Machines (3 entries — frame disposal with active instances, sub-cache hit inside microstep, machine spawn before adapter ready); Machines x SSR (2 — allowed-subset behaviour, hydration with snapshots); Routing x SSR (2 — routing in SSR, route-not-found status); Frames x Reactive Substrate (3 — disposal during render, no-context fallback, plain-fn warning); Machines x Errors (3 — action throws, fx throws inside action, hot-reload mid-flight); Drain loop x Substrate (1 — re-entrant render dispatch); Machines x Tooling (1 — Tool-Pair time-travel); Errors x SSR (2 — error projection, machine error in SSR); Subscriptions x Hot-reload (1 — mid-cascade re-registration); Stories x Testing (1 — id-valued fx-overrides portability); Boot x Substrate (1 — adapter swap forbidden). Each entry: Specs that meet, Scenario, Behaviour, Reason, Conformance fixture pointer. Plus a How to read section, When to update guidance, and cross-refs to Runtime-Architecture and conformance corpus. Wired into README.md companion-docs index.

title: Specify hot-reload surgical-update semantics for re-registration
description:
Hot reload is mentioned across the docs (a primary developer-experience promise; one of the things re-frame v1 does well). re-frame2 inherits this but the surgical-update algorithm -- exactly what happens when an event handler, sub, fx, machine, etc. is re-registered while the app is running -- has not been written down. An AI implementing CLJS will probably implement clear-and-replace in the registry, which works for handlers but not for machines (active machine instances reference the old handler closure) or for caches (cached subs hold stale derive-fns).

CONTENTS:

A specification of the surgical-update behaviour for each registry kind:

- :event handlers -- replace registry slot; in-flight events keep the old handler (run-to-completion); next dispatch sees new
- :sub handlers -- replace registry slot; cached sub values invalidate; next deref recomputes through the new derive-fn
- :fx handlers -- replace registry slot; in-flight :fx sequences finish using the resolved (old) handler; new dispatches see new
- :cofx handlers -- replace registry slot; same in-flight rule
- :machine-actions and :machine-guards -- replace registry slot; the question: do active machine instances continue using the old action or pick up the new? Probably new on next microstep, but spec needs to say so
- Machines themselves (re-register-machine of a different definition while instances are running) -- options: stop and restart instances, vs let them run to natural termination; spec needs to choose
- :route handlers -- replace registry slot; current navigation continues; next navigation uses new
- :head handlers -- replace registry slot; SSR not affected (request-scoped frame); CSR re-renders pick up new

For each: the rule, the why, what edge cases break.

Plus a §Hot-reload contract section stating:
- Re-registration is non-destructive to in-flight work (run-to-completion)
- Cached values invalidate on relevant re-registration
- Active machine instances behave [insert chosen rule] on action/guard re-registration
- The trace bus emits a :hot-reload event so devtools can react
- During a hot reload window, dispatch is not paused -- the system stays live

V1 REFERENCE:
re-frame v1 has working hot-reload via clear-handlers and re-registration; the algorithm is mostly run-to-completion + replace registry slot + invalidate sub cache. What is NEW for re-frame2: machines (active instances + replace action/guard), the explicit run-to-completion contract, the trace event for hot reload.

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
Hot reload is one of the affordances every CLJS dev expects. An AI given no spec for it will either skip it (silently breaking shadow-cljs reload behaviour) or implement clear-and-replace which subtly breaks machines.

FILES:
- New: docs/specification/Hot-Reload.md companion (cross-referenced from 001-Registration, 005-StateMachines, 002-Frames), OR
- New §Hot reload section in 001-Registration with cross-refs from 005 and 002

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (primary), rf2-t059 (Runtime-Architecture.md must mention hot-reload as a property of the registry component), rf2-l2ck (drain loop -- run-to-completion guarantee comes from the loop)

WHY P1: hot reload is a baseline DX promise; getting it wrong silently breaks shadow-cljs flow. Estimated effort: small focused write -- can borrow from v1.
close_reason: Replaced thin §Hot-reload semantics in 001-Registration with full contract section. Five contract guarantees (non-destructive to in-flight work, cached values invalidate, machine instances pick up actions on next microstep, trace event emitted, dispatch not paused). Per-kind rules table for all 13 registry kinds (event, sub, fx, cofx, machine-action, machine-guard, machine-def, view, frame, app-schema, route, head, error-projector) listing what re-registration replaces, in-flight behaviour, cache disposal, trace event. §Re-registration of a different function — collision warning section keeping v1 last-write-wins behaviour but adding :rf.warning/registration-collision detection. §How registrations interact with active machine instances clarifying the three reference paths (machine def, machine-scoped actions, globally-registered actions) and which trigger re-spawn vs apply on next microstep. §Hot-reload trace surface with stable :registry/handler-replaced shape including :reason and per-kind extras (:active-instances, :disposed-slots). §Edge cases (mid-cascade re-registration, frame :on-create not re-firing, destroyed-then-re-registered frame, prod-build hot-reload). v1 source named explicitly as the implementation reference. Cross-refs added: 002 §Re-registration — surgical update points to 001 for non-frame kinds; Runtime-Architecture.md updated to reference the now-locked section.

title: Document sub-cache contract: invalidation algorithm and lifecycle
description:
The sub-cache is referenced across 002 (frames hold a cache), 005 (subs see machine state), 006 (substrate reactions wrap cache values), 010 (schemas mention :rf/sub-cache shape) but no single place specifies the invalidation algorithm, the reference-counting / lifetime rules, or what happens on app-db change. v1 has a working sub-cache implementation in re-frame.subs; re-frame2 needs to surface the algorithm explicitly so non-CLJS implementors and the CLJS one-shotter can build it without reverse-engineering.

CONTENTS:

Specifically: a §Sub-cache section (probably in 002 or as a standalone Sub-Cache.md companion) covering:

1. Cache shape -- per-frame map keyed by [query-vector] storing {:value v :reaction r :ref-count n :inputs-fingerprint f}
2. Lookup -- (subscribe q) returns existing cache slot if present; otherwise computes, stores, returns
3. Invalidation algorithm:
   - On app-db change, traverse cache; for each entry whose computed value depends on changed paths, recompute lazily on next deref
   - Reagent does this naturally via reactions; for non-reactive substrates (or test substrate), pseudocode the equivalent
4. Reference counting and disposal:
   - Subs registered as :reg-sub-by-path are eagerly cached
   - Subs with no live readers can be disposed (per substrate); the cache must release the entry
5. Layer-1 vs Layer-2 vs Layer-3 sub semantics (v1 framing):
   - Layer-1: direct reactions on app-db
   - Layer-2: reactions over Layer-1 subs
   - Layer-3: dynamic / parameterised
   - Re-frame2 keeps the layer concept but the spec needs to say so explicitly
6. Lifetime contract: when a frame is disposed, all cached subs are disposed; no leaks

V1 REFERENCE:
re-frame.subs has the working algorithm. The sub-cache invalidation is mostly v1s algorithm. The new thing for re-frame2: per-frame cache (v1 has a single global cache); the layer-1/2/3 distinction documented in spec rather than implicit; the disposal-on-frame-dispose contract.

The pseudocode does not need to be a full implementation; it needs to be specific enough that a non-CLJS implementor can write the equivalent in their host. For CLJS, "uses Reagent reactions per Spec 006 §Reference adapter" is enough; the algorithm matters more for non-CLJS hosts.

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
Without this, an AI implementing CLJS will probably wire Reagent reactions and call it a day -- which works on the happy path but misses the per-frame disposal and ref-counting contract. With it, the disposal-correctness is explicit.

FILES:
- 002-Frames.md: new §Sub-cache section, OR
- New: docs/specification/Sub-Cache.md companion (cross-referenced from 002 and 006)

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (primary), rf2-t059 (Runtime-Architecture.md mentions sub-cache as a component -- this is the contract for that component), rf2-i3q9 (Reagent adapter pseudocode shows the CLJS realisation -- this bead specifies the contract the realisation satisfies)

WHY P1: gates correct implementation of frame disposal (a Goal-2 surface) and substrate-portability. Estimated effort: small focused write; can lean heavily on v1 source.
close_reason: Expanded 006 §Subscription cache invalidation — operational semantics into a full §Subscription cache — contract and operational semantics covering: cache shape (per-frame map keyed by query-vector with :value :derived-container :inputs :ref-count :on-dispose); lookup algorithm with cache-key dedup and recursive :<- chain resolution; invalidation algorithm in three phases (layer-1 → layer-2+ topological cascade → subscriber notify) host-agnostic with explicit fallback for non-Reagent hosts; layer-1/2/3 sub semantics named explicitly with table; reference counting and disposal with on-dispose hook; lifetime contract — frame disposal cascade; cross-spec interactions (drain-loop step 2 timing, hot-reload, machine subscriptions); per-host implementation notes (Reagent, headless/SSR, Solid, generic). v1 source named explicitly as the implementation reference; what is new in v2 (per-frame caches, contractualised disposal, explicit layer framing) is called out.

title: Add drain-loop pseudocode to 002-Frames
description:
Spec 005-StateMachines defines four Levels of run-to-completion semantics (1: synchronous handlers; 2: machine microsteps via :always; 3: subscribed views recomputing inside the same drain step; 4: external/async work yielding back to the loop). The semantics are described in prose. An AI implementing CLJS has to translate four prose-described levels into a working drain loop -- which is a non-trivial leap. This bead adds explicit drain-loop pseudocode in 002 (or a new Drain-Loop appendix) that shows the actual loop structure: dequeue, process envelope, run handler, apply effects (including :raise re-enqueue), settle machines via :always, fire subscribers, repeat until queue empty.

CONTENTS:

A pseudocode block of roughly 60-100 lines showing:
- The outer drain loop (dispatched until empty)
- Per-event: pull envelope, run interceptor chain, invoke handler, collect effects map
- :fx interpretation in source order, with do-fx reentrancy rules
- :raise effect: enqueue at front (vs :dispatch enqueue at back)
- Machine settling: between events (or between :raise items?), call run-always-actions on every active machine until quiescent
- Sub-cache invalidation: triggered by app-db replace; subscribers fire after drain step settles
- Trace bus: when each phase emits trace events
- Error trapping: where on-error projector is called

Plus an inline numbered list mapping each line back to the four Levels in 005.

Plus the small set of edge cases:
- :raise inside a machine action that itself runs in :always
- An event dispatched from a render (re-entrant dispatch -- queued, not synchronous)
- Effects that call back into dispatch (-fx returning :dispatch -- normal queue path)
- Frame disposal mid-drain (event is in flight; what happens?)

V1 REFERENCE:
re-frame v1 has a working router and event-loop; the drain loop in re-frame.router.cljc is the reference. What is NEW for re-frame2 and warrants explicit pseudocode:
- :raise (front-of-queue dispatch) is a new effect not in v1 baseline
- Machine integration (:always microsteps interleaved with drain) is new
- The four Levels framing in 005 is new framing of v1 mechanics
- The sub-cache settling order between drain events vs after the entire batch -- this needs to be explicit
- Run-to-completion guarantees in the presence of :raise and machine microsteps need explicit prose

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
The drain loop is the single most subtle piece of runtime to get right. An AI given prose Levels will probably implement something that works for the happy path but breaks on machine settling or :raise ordering. Pseudocode collapses the design space.

FILES:
- 002-Frames.md: new §Drain loop (pseudocode) section, OR
- New: docs/specification/Drain-Loop.md companion (cross-referenced from 002 and 005)

Decision on file location can happen in the implementation; new section in 002 is the simpler default.

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (primary motivation), rf2-t059 (Runtime-Architecture.md -- the diagram-level companion to this pseudocode-level), rf2-vf8m (other-host implementors need this too)

WHY P1: gates one-shot implementation. The most error-prone runtime piece. Estimated effort: medium focused write; reading v1 router source helps.
close_reason: Added §Drain-loop pseudocode subsection inside 002 §Run-to-completion dispatch (drain semantics), placed after §:fx ordering and atomicity guarantees and before §Per-frame and per-call overrides. Roughly 130 lines of pseudocode in three blocks: (1) outer drain (Level 4) with next-tick scheduling and depth-limit guard; (2) per-event drain showing interceptor chain → :db → sub-cache invalidate → :fx walk with error trapping; (3) machine event handler showing the Level-3 cascade — raise queue drain, :always microstep loop, single snapshot commit. Plus a do-fx skeleton for the four reserved fx-ids the runtime owns. Plus an Interaction map cross-referencing every component the drain touches (registrar, substrate adapter, sub-cache, trace bus, error contract, machine cascade) and five edge cases (:raise inside :always, re-entrant render dispatch, :dispatch-to-self vs :raise, frame disposal mid-drain, async fx semantics). v1 router named explicitly as the implementation reference.

title: Add Reagent reference-adapter pseudocode to 006-ReactiveSubstrate
description:
006-ReactiveSubstrate defines the adapter contract abstractly (make-state-container, subscribe-container, make-derived-value, render, render-to-string, register-context-provider, dispose-adapter). The contract is right but the bridge from contract to a working Reagent implementation is left for the AI to invent. The CLJS reference adapter -- Reagent on top of React -- has well-known patterns (r/atom for state container, r/track! for subscription, r/reaction for derived value, r/render for mount); this bead adds a §Reference adapter pseudocode appendix that shows how each contract function maps to a Reagent primitive.

CONTENTS:

For each adapter contract function, ~10-15 lines of Clojure-flavoured pseudocode showing the Reagent realisation. Roughly:

- make-state-container -> wraps a clojure.core/atom or reagent.core/atom; returns a value the runtime treats opaquely.
- read-container -> deref.
- replace-container! -> reset! (with the structural-sharing PDS guarantee).
- subscribe-container -> r/track! over a reaction; returns an unsubscribe fn.
- make-derived-value -> r/reaction; the body is the derive-fn.
- render -> reagent.dom.client/create-root + render; returns an unmount-fn.
- render-to-string -> reagent.dom.server/render-to-string (JVM-runnable).
- register-context-provider -> a thin wrapper using react-context that the frame-provider uses.
- dispose-adapter! -> dispose all reactions, unsubscribe all listeners, dissoc the registry slot.

Plus key implementation notes:
- How to wire Reagent ratoms to the framers app-db so subs see updates.
- How sub-cache integrates with r/reaction lifecycle (track!/dispose!).
- How frame-provider uses React context (and how plain-fn-under-non-default-frame warning fires).
- How the JVM-side adapter (plain-atom) differs.

V1 REFERENCE:
re-frame v1 has working Reagent integration in re-frame.interop and re-frame.subs. An AI implementing CLJS can lean on v1 source for the patterns. What is NEW for re-frame2 and worth pseudocoding: the adapter-contract boundary itself (substrate decoupling work in 006); the disposal lifecycle (re-frame v1 does not have explicit adapter disposal); the rule that adapter-internal state must be derivable from frame value (Goal 2 / rf2-edi resolved).

The pseudocode does not need to be a full impl -- just enough that the AI knows which Reagent primitives bridge to which contract functions and what the wiring looks like.

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
Without this, the AI has to know Reagent well enough to invent the bridging. With it, the AI can produce the adapter from pseudocode and a Reagent dependency.

FILES:
- 006-ReactiveSubstrate.md: new §Reference adapter (pseudocode) appendix
- Possibly 002-Frames.md: cross-reference from the View ergonomics section

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (one-shot bar -- primary motivation), rf2-edi (closed; revertibility audit -- documents the adapter-internal state rule), rf2-vf8m (multi-impl -- a JS impl will write its own adapter; the spec needs to show what the contract realisation looks like for one host).

WHY P1: gates one-shot CLJS implementation; without the bridge the contract floats. Estimated effort: small focused write -- ~100 lines of pseudocode + a few notes.
close_reason: Replaced the thin §CLJS reference: Reagent as default adapter section in 006 with full bridging pseudocode. Per-contract-fn pseudocode for each of the nine adapter functions (make-state-container, read-container, replace-container!, subscribe-container, make-derived-value, render, render-to-string, register-context-provider, dispose-adapter!) with inline notes on what each Reagent primitive is doing. Plus three cross-cutting subsections: §Sub-cache wiring (how reg-sub registrations realise as Reagent reactions, with v1-source-as-reference annotation, hot-reload and frame-teardown behaviour spelled out); §Frame-provider via React context (the provider component, plain-fn-under-non-default-frame warning detection, cross-ref to 002 for the read-side); §Plain-atom adapter (JVM, SSR, headless) (how it differs — no caching, render throws, no context provider, trivially revertibility-compliant). Plus §Adapter selection at boot. v1 source is named explicitly as the implementation reference. Cross-references to 002, 008, 011, 000 verified.

title: Add Runtime-Architecture.md — birds-eye view that ties the runtime components together
description:
No single doc currently shows the runtime as a coherent whole. The runtime is described piecewise across 002 (frames), 005 (machines), 006 (substrate), 009 (instrumentation), 010 (schemas). An AI implementing CLJS has to assemble the picture from fragments. A one-page Runtime-Architecture.md companion doc gives the missing birds-eye view.

CONTENTS:

A single page describing runtime components and relationships.

Components:
- The registry (per-kind: :event, :sub, :fx, :cofx, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :route, :head, :error-projector)
- The router (event queue per frame; FIFO; drain loop)
- The drain loop (the four Levels in 005, how it processes one event end-to-end)
- The frame container (one atom per frame holding app-db plus runtime state)
- The sub-cache (per-frame cache of computed sub values; invalidated by app-db changes)
- The trace bus (per-process trace event stream; listeners batched at debounce window)
- The fx interpreter (do-fx walks :fx vector in source order)
- The interop layer (re-frame.interop -- clock primitives, native scheduling, host-clock now-ms)

For each component: inputs and outputs, key invariants, how other components see it, one-line cross-reference to the canonical Spec.

Plus a single diagram (ASCII art works) showing the data flow:
dispatch -> envelope -> router -> drain -> handler -> effects -> :db write -> sub-cache invalidate -> view re-render.

V1 REFERENCE:
re-frame v1 has a working runtime; an AI implementing CLJS can read v1 source for component implementation. What is NEW for re-frame2 and worth documenting explicitly: frames as a layer, machines integration into the dispatch pipeline, the trace surface as a first-class component, the substrate adapter boundary. v1 does not need a runtime-architecture doc because the code IS the doc; for re-frame2 (especially non-CLJS implementors per rf2-vf8m), the doc has to exist.

LEVERAGE FOR rf2-5pt:
Probably the highest-leverage missing artefact. Without it, an AI implementing has to reverse-engineer the runtime from contracts and prose. With it, the AI has the architecture diagram in front of them.

FILES:
- New: docs/specification/Runtime-Architecture.md (Type: Reference)
- README.md companion-docs index: add to Companion layer
- Optional: 002, 005, 006, 009 each gain a one-line cross-reference at top

PAIRS WITH:
rf2-5pt (AI-implementable goal -- primary motivation), rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org -- essential for re-frame-js, re-frame-py), rf2-13lo (Implementor Checklist -- runtime architecture is the conceptual frame Part-2 decisions sit inside)

WHY P1: gates one-shot implementation. Estimated effort: medium focused doc-write pass.
close_reason: Created docs/specification/Runtime-Architecture.md as Reference / Companion. Eight components (registrar, frame container, router, drain loop, do-fx, sub-cache, substrate adapter, trace bus) plus the interop layer, each with one-paragraph role + inputs/outputs/invariants + owning Spec cross-ref. ASCII data-flow diagram. Three lifecycles (boot, per-event drain, teardown). Whats new vs re-frame v1 table mapping each component to inherited vs new behaviour for non-CLJS implementors. Cross-referenced from README companion-docs index and from the abstracts of 002/005/006/009.

title: First-class HTTP response contract for SSR — close the response-correctness gap
description:
The current 011-SSR.md spec has :rf.server/set-status but doesn't treat the full HTTP response as a first-class output. Modern SSR frameworks (Next.js, React Router, Astro, Nuxt, SvelteKit) all expose status / headers / cookies / redirect as explicit response shapes that handlers can populate during SSR.

Without this, re-frame2 SSR is 'HTML + state' but not 'full HTTP response' — incomplete for production use cases that need correct 404/401/302/500 behavior, cache-control, Set-Cookie, redirects, etc.

## Concrete benefit

- Correct HTTP status codes for SSR pages (404, 401, 302, 500).
- Cache-control and other response headers.
- Set-Cookie support.
- Redirects as part of render/load control flow.
- Portable adapter boundary across server hosts (Express, Ring, Fastify, etc.).

## Fit with re-frame2

- It's data, not magic — fits the data-over-magic ethos.
- Response policy stays enumerable and testable.
- Matches the per-request frame model — each request frame owns its response shape.
- Avoids pushing server behavior into host-specific glue code.
- Respects the event/fx architecture: response is just another explicit output of the request frame.

## Mechanism

A response accumulator owned by the request frame:

  {:rf/response
   {:status   200
    :headers  {"content-type" "text/html; charset=utf-8"}
    :cookies  []
    :redirect nil}}

Standard server-only fx:

- :rf.server/set-status      — set the response status code
- :rf.server/set-header       — replace a header (case-insensitive)
- :rf.server/append-header    — append (e.g., Set-Cookie can have multiple)
- :rf.server/set-cookie       — structured cookie map (name, value, max-age, secure, etc.)
- :rf.server/delete-cookie    — clear a named cookie
- :rf.server/redirect         — set status + Location; truncates HTML render

The request handler returns a structured result:

  {:html     "<!doctype html>..."
   :payload  hydration-payload
   :response response-map}

The host adapter consumes :response to build the actual HTTP response.

## Open design calls

1. **Redirect precedence over normal HTML render.** If a handler emits :rf.server/redirect mid-render, does the HTML get truncated and only the redirect goes out? Lean: yes — redirects take precedence over rendering, and the runtime emits no HTML body when :redirect is set.

2. **Multiple status setters.** If two handlers in the drain both emit :rf.server/set-status, last-write-wins or first-write-wins or warn? Lean: last-write-wins (standard re-frame semantics for :db) but emit a :rf.warning/multiple-status-set trace.

3. **Header replacement vs append.** :rf.server/set-header replaces; :rf.server/append-header adds another instance. Document both explicitly. Deduplication is left to the host adapter.

4. **Cookie shape.** Structured maps ("good") vs raw header strings ("escape hatch")? Lean: structured maps as canonical with a known shape; the host adapter serialises to wire format. Avoids cookie-attribute quoting bugs.

5. **Status defaults.** Implicit default 200 when no handler sets a status; explicit Content-Type header default; what content-type for error pages?

## Files to update when actioning

- 011-SSR.md: new §HTTP response contract; document the :rf/response slot; document the six standard fx; document the request-handler return shape; document the open-call resolutions.
- Spec-Schemas.md: add :rf/response schema; add :rf.server/cookie schema; six fx-args schemas.
- API.md: add the six :rf.server/* fx rows; add the :rf/response sub.
- conformance/fixtures/: minimum 3 fixtures: ssr-redirect.edn (handler emits redirect; verify :status 302 and :location header), ssr-set-status.edn (handler emits 404; verify :status 404), ssr-cookie.edn (handler sets a cookie; verify :set-cookie header in response).
- 009-Instrumentation.md: new :rf.warning/multiple-status-set warning category.

## Why P1 (not P2)

This is correctness for the AI-implementability goal (rf2-5pt). A JS or Python implementor of re-frame2 shouldn't have to invent the response contract; the spec defines it. Without it, conformance is 'renders HTML' but not 'serves HTTP correctly' — a real production gap.

## Pairs with

- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot the implementation) — closes a major gap in the SSR contract.
- rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org) — re-frame-cljs and re-frame-js need the same response contract for cross-impl conformance.
- 011 existing :rf.server/set-status — extends, doesn't replace.

Status: open. Design largely locked; five open calls flagged for resolution at action time. Estimated effort: medium focused agent pass.
close_reason: Specced HTTP response contract for SSR: :rf/response accumulator, six :rf.server/* fx, request-result shape, locked redirect/multi-status/header/cookie/default behaviours; schemas, API rows, three conformance fixtures, multiple-status-set warning.

title: Navigation epoch — suppress stale async results from superseded routes
description:
When a route is loading and the user navigates away before the load completes, the old loads result can land after the user has moved on, clobbering newer state. This is a real bug class that React Router and TanStack Router both explicitly handle. The current 012-Routing.md spec doesnt address it.

This is the SAME PATTERN as the epoch-based stale-detection just locked for `:after` (rf2-f6g5, closed). The runtime increments a counter on state-change; async events carry the counter at scheduling time; on receipt, the runtime checks current vs carried; mismatch → ignored. The pattern travels naturally from `:after` to navigation.

## Concrete benefit

- Closes a real correctness bug: older loads cant overwrite newer state.
- No new cancellation primitive needed — staleness detection is enough for the user-visible bug.
- Hosts that DO support abortable fetches can also abort superseded work — but suppression alone fixes the visible problem.

## Mechanism

When a route becomes active, the runtime allocates a navigation token (epoch). Each `:on-match` dispatch receives the token in coeffects:

```clojure
{:route/id :route/article
 :params   {:id "123"}
 :query    {}
 :transition :loading
 :nav-token "nav-42"}
```

Async completions must either:
1. Carry the token in their follow-up event, or
2. Use framework-provided fx wrappers that attach it automatically.

If the carried token no longer matches the current route token, the result is ignored (and emits a `:route.nav-token/stale-suppressed` trace event for debugging — same pattern as `:machine.timer/stale-after` in `:after`).

Hosts that support abortable fetches MAY also abort in-flight work for superseded tokens. Conformance only requires suppression of stale commits.

## Implementation sketch

- `:rf/url-changed` event allocates a new `:nav-token` (gensym or counter).
- Route slice in app-db gains `:nav-token`:
  ```clojure
  {:id :route/article
   :params {:id "123"}
   :query  {}
   :transition :loading
   :nav-token "nav-42"}
  ```
- `:on-match` events receive the token in coeffects (`(:nav-token (:cofx ctx))`).
- A new fx wrapper `:rf.route/with-nav-token` automatically threads the token into the dispatched continuation event:
  ```clojure
  {:fx [[:rf.route/with-nav-token
         {:do [:dispatch [:my-handler ...]]
          :nav-token current-token}]]}
  ```
- On the receiving handler, framework-provided cofx checks the carried token against the current route token; mismatch raises `:rf.route/nav-token-stale` (silent — emits trace, no transition).

## Files to update when actioning

- 012-Routing.md: new §"Navigation tokens — stale-result suppression"; document the epoch allocation, token-in-cofx, the fx wrapper, the trace event.
- Spec-Schemas.md: extend `:rf/route-slice` with `:nav-token`; add the wrapper fx schema.
- conformance/fixtures/: new fixture `route-stale-nav-token-suppression.edn` — load route A; navigate to route B before A finishes; A finishes; verify the late result is suppressed; verify the trace shows `:route.nav-token/stale-suppressed`.
- 009-Instrumentation.md: add the new trace event type.

## Why P1

Correctness, not polish. This is a real bug class users hit constantly in production. Re-frame2 should ship with this story locked.

## Pairs with

- rf2-f6g5 (closed; `:after` epoch-based stale detection) — same idiom; this bead applies it to navigation.
- See also the cross-cutting "async stale-detection pattern" bead for the meta-pattern across `:after`, navigation, and future async features.
- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot) — without this, the conformance bar for "route loading" is broken.

Status: open. Design locked; ready to action. Estimated effort: medium focused agent pass.
close_reason: Added 012-Routing.md §Navigation tokens — stale-result suppression; nav-token in :rf/route-slice; :rf.route/with-nav-token fx wrapper; trace events :route.nav-token/allocated and :route.nav-token/stale-suppressed; conformance fixture route-stale-nav-token-suppression.edn.

title: Deterministic route ranking — close URL-match correctness gap
description:
The current 012-Routing.md spec defines the path grammar (path syntax, params, rest segments) but does NOT specify a full ranking rule for when multiple registered routes could match the same URL. This is a correctness gap that directly blocks the AI-one-shot goal (rf2-5pt) — two implementations of `match-url` can both be "reasonable" and still disagree on which route wins for an ambiguous URL.

Mature routers solve this by ranking specificity:
- TanStack Router sorts by route-pattern specificity at registration.
- Vue Router supports expressive matching and therefore needs deterministic resolution.

## Concrete benefit

- Strengthens the pure `match-url` contract — same URL, same registrations, same match across implementations.
- Improves cross-host conformance — a CLJS impl and a JS impl produce identical results on the conformance corpus.
- Helps AI generation — route conflicts become predictable instead of relying on registration-order folklore.
- Catches developer mistakes — registering routes that shadow each other can be detected at registration time.

## Locked ranking algorithm (proposed)

When multiple registered routes match the same URL, rank by:

1. **More static segments beat fewer static segments.** `/users/me` beats `/users/:id`.
2. **Among equally-static-counted matches, longer paths beat shorter.** `/users/:id/edit` beats `/users/:id`.
3. **Named params beat rest params.** `/files/:name` beats `/files/*rest`.
4. **Rest params beat catch-all/not-found routes.** `/files/*rest` beats `/*`.
5. **Exact routes beat routes that rely on optional groups.** `/about` beats `/{:base}?/about`.
6. **Registration order is the final tiebreak only if every structural score is equal.** Discouraged — a warning at registration time when two routes have equal scores.

## Implementation

- `match-url` MUST use this ranking. Spec the algorithm in pseudocode in 012.
- The conformance corpus gets ambiguous-path fixtures: registrations with overlapping patterns; verify the same URL matches the same route across implementations.
- Tooling can warn at registration when routes have equal scores or when one shadows another.

## Files to update when actioning

- 012-Routing.md: new §"Route ranking algorithm" with the 6-rule cascade; pseudocode; warning category for shadow detection.
- Spec-Schemas.md: optionally add `:rf/route-rank` schema if the rank is exposed via tooling.
- conformance/fixtures/: new fixture `route-ranking-precedence.edn` with an ambiguous-pattern set; verify deterministic match.
- API.md: add new `:rf.warning/route-shadowed-by-equal-score` warning category.

## Why P1

This is correctness, not polish. Without the ranking lock, two implementations cannot be expected to agree, which defeats the point of cross-impl conformance. The fix is small (a defined algorithm); the win is large (deterministic cross-host behaviour).

## Pairs with

- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot the implementation) — the conformance corpus is the verification engine; route ranking is one of the determinism requirements.
- rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org) — when re-frame-cljs and re-frame-js both ship, ranking is what keeps them in sync.

Status: open. Design locked; ready to action. Estimated effort: small focused agent pass.
close_reason: Locked 6-rule ranking cascade in 012-Routing.md §Route ranking algorithm; added :rf/route-rank schema; new :rf.warning/route-shadowed-by-equal-score category; conformance fixture route-ranking-precedence.edn.

title: Hierarchical compound states — substantive design work (drain-rule extensions, transition resolution across levels)
description:
Spawned from rf2-g1h. The capability matrix in 005 claims hierarchical compound states for v1, but the substantive design work is not landed in this pass. Required:

1. Drain-rule extensions for cascading entry/exit along the path (entering :a.b.c fires :a/entry then :b/entry then :c/entry; leaving fires :c/exit then :b/exit then :a/exit). Extends Level 2 of the four-level drain.
2. Deep state-id resolution semantics — :state field as either a flat keyword (current) or a vector / dotted-keyword path (post-extension). Snapshot shape extension.
3. Transition resolution across compound levels — bubbling: a transition for an event in a child state, if not handled, looks up at the parent. Precedence rules.
4. Schema additions in Spec-Schemas.md for nested :states.
5. Conformance fixtures: hierarchical-entry-exit, hierarchical-deep-target, hierarchical-transition-resolution.

Pairs with rf2-g1h (capability matrix) which claimed the capability without landing the substantive work.
close_reason: Hierarchical compound states specified: snapshot dual form, LCA-based entry/exit cascading, deepest-wins parent fallthrough, target resolution (vector + relative-keyword), schema additions in Spec-Schemas, three conformance fixtures. Substantive content in 005 §Hierarchical compound states.

The current docs still disagree between `#{:client}` and universal `#{:server :client}`, which materially changes legacy fx/cofx behavior under SSR and headless execution.

title: Adapter API contract has incomplete signatures (callbacks, teardown, disposal)

Action: write concrete signatures with arg/return types for all 5 functions; specify the disposal lifecycle (when does an adapter shut down, who calls it, what happens to in-flight effects).

Added error categories :rf.error/derived-container-replaced and :rf.error/adapter-disposed to API.md.

title: :platforms #{:client} default silently breaks SSR-shared fx
description: HIGH (backwards-compat). 011-SSR:135 declares :platforms defaults to #{:client} when absent. v1 apps register fx without :platforms. Any fx that runs on JVM headless tests or shared CLJC code would now be silently skipped (with :rf.fx/skipped-on-platform trace, not error). Not covered in MIGRATION.md. Action: either (a) change default to #{:server :client} (break-but-explicit; new fx must declare :platforms #{:client} to opt in to client-only); (b) add migration rule that explicitly lists every fx without :platforms as a Type-B human-review (review whether it should be #{:client} or #{:server :client} or #{:client :server}). Lean: (a) — safer default for migrating apps; new code follows the pattern's discipline of explicit :platforms anyway. Update 011 + MIGRATION.
close_reason: Default :platforms changed from #{:client} to #{:server :client} (universal). Matches v1's effective behaviour — fx ran wherever dispatched. Migrating apps don't get silent skipping. New fx that genuinely cannot run server-side (browser-DOM, localStorage, requireJS-window) declare :platforms #{:client} explicitly. Updated 011-SSR §:platforms metadata in two places (overview + S-3 detailed design). MIGRATION.md 'What stays the same' lists reg-fx/reg-cofx without :platforms as preserved-and-universal.

title: Kind-vocabulary inconsistency: :event vs :event-db/:event-fx vs :machine across 001/002/005
description: HIGH. (a) 001-Registration:114 query API table lists kinds as :event, :sub, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :route, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. (b) 002-Frames:784 uses :event-db, :event-fx, :event-ctx, :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :interceptor. (c) 005-StateMachines:230 uses (rf/handlers :machine), but 001 omits :machine entirely. AI implementer cannot tell what kinds the registrar accepts. Action: pick canonical vocabulary (lean: 001's flat :event/:sub/:fx; introduce :machine as a kind, not just :machine-action/:machine-guard) and propagate everywhere.
close_reason: Kind-vocabulary canonicalised. 001 §The query API now has a canonical-kind table: :event (covers all 3 reg-event-*; sub-kind on metadata as :event/kind ∈ {:db :fx :ctx}), :sub, :sub-raw, :fx, :cofx, :view, :frame, :interceptor, :route, :machine, :machine-action, :machine-guard, :app-schema. 002 §Public registrar query API now points at 001 for the canonical list rather than enumerating its own (different) version. AI implementer has one definitive source.

title: EffectMap closure consistency: examples violate the closed contract; M-7/O-7/'stays-the-same' say different things
description: HIGH. Spec-Schemas:111 declares :rf/effect-map :closed with only :db and :fx, but: (a) 002-Frames:401-405 worked example returns {:http {...}} top-level; (b) 005-StateMachines:147 machine actions example returns {:dispatch ... :http ...}; (c) MIGRATION M-7 (line 220-280) says all top-level non-:db keys move to :fx and supersedes O-7, BUT MIGRATION 'What stays the same' (line 495) says :dispatch-n is deprecated-but-works, AND O-7 (line 401) describes :dispatch-n as preserved. AI agent migrating cannot tell whether :dispatch-n is hard-break or kept. Also: closed-by-default conflicts with open-shapes-everywhere principle (Best-Practice review). Also: M-7 says 'any user-registered fx that was previously called as a top-level key' but no mechanical pattern for discovering them. Action: (1) fix the 002 and 005 examples; (2) reconcile M-7 vs O-7 vs stays-the-same — make :dispatch-n a hard-break with O-7 absorbed into M-7; (3) document the migration agent's discovery procedure (sweep reg-fx ids); (4) reconsider whether EffectMap should be open with documented known keys (consistent with everywhere-else) — proposal: keep closed, since this is a contract between handler and runtime, but add this rationale in Spec-Schemas.
close_reason: EffectMap closure made consistent. (1) Fixed worked example in 002 §Async effects (top-level :http now inside :fx). (2) Fixed worked example in 005 §Actions (top-level :dispatch / :http now inside :fx; merge-strategy prose updated to match — fx vectors concatenate). (3) Reconciled :dispatch-n: M-7 now the canonical rewrite rule; O-7 marked absorbed-into-M-7 with a pointer; What-stays-the-same updated to say only :db and :fx are top-level effect-map keys. (4) M-7 now includes a mechanical discovery procedure: sweep reg-fx ids first, then rewrite per-handler; unknown top-level keys flag for human review. (5) Intro 'covers five cases' updated to 'six cases'.

title: Run-to-completion drain (M-3) is Type B but classified Type A — silent breakage risk
description: HIGH. C1 in 000-Vision says: a breaking change is acceptable if Detectable + Mechanically-rewritable + Behaviour-preserving. M-3 (MIGRATION:87-126) admits the rewrite 'may need behavioural review, not a mechanical rewrite' and depends on intent — that's Type B. But MIGRATION:281 lists M-3 alongside fully-mechanical rules in the 'sweep is complete' check. Per C1: 'Side-effects can be silently reordered with observable consequences' is a design failure. Action: explicitly tag M-3 as Type B (flag-for-human-review). Update MIGRATION's sweep-complete check at line 281 to acknowledge that M-3 hits require human review, even after detection.
close_reason: M-3 explicitly marked Type B at the sweep-complete summary in MIGRATION. Rule list now distinguishes Type A (M-1, M-4, M-5, M-6, M-7) — agent rewrites mechanically — from Type B (M-3) — agent flags + asks before rewriting. Per C1 a breaking change with silent reordering is a design failure; M-3's classification now matches that contract. Migration-complete gate now requires both Type-A auto-rewrites pass AND Type-B human-approval recorded.

title: 011-SSR is too incomplete for v1-required
description: Multiple core protocol decisions still open as 'Lean:' answers in §Open questions: S-2 hydration-mismatch contract, S-3 server-side effect handling beyond the basic platforms gate, S-4 JVM-runnable view rendering details, S-5 routing-and-SSR specifics, S-6 auth/session at the SSR layer, S-7 hydration of non-state runtime artefacts. The status banner still says 'Skeleton. v1-required. Full design to follow.' Resolve each open question with a concrete decision or bump 011 from v1-required if the design isn't ready.
close_reason: 011-SSR open questions S-2..S-7 all resolved with concrete decisions. S-2 hydration mismatch: structured trace event + warn-and-replace default, strict-mode opt-in. S-3 server fx: :platforms metadata + skipped-on-platform trace, default :client-only. S-4 JVM rendering: pure hiccup → string, no JVM React, no server-side lifecycle. S-5 routing: :rf/server-init dispatches :route/handle-url-change with request URL; same handler universal. S-6 auth: cofx injection from request session at :rf/server-init time; no SSR-specific surface. S-7 non-state hydration: v1 ships only :app-db hydration; sub-warmups etc. post-v1. Status banner moved from 'Skeleton' to 'Drafting'.

title: Migrate reorient.md content into permanent docs (so reorient can be removed)
description:
Goal: be able to delete reorient.md without losing content. The following substantive content currently lives ONLY in reorient.md and would need to move to permanent docs first:

1. **Identity primitive — required properties + per-host realisations** (reorient §The identity primitive lines 118-144). The 7 properties (stable, namespaceable, value-equal, cheap, serialisable, human-readable, reflective) and the per-host table (CLJS keywords, TS branded strings, Python Id-class, Kotlin sealed-classes, Rust newtype). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern, or new Spec 001 once drafted.

2. **What the pattern should NOT over-commit to** (reorient §What the pattern should NOT over-commit to lines 146-159). Full bullet list (macros, Vars, Reagent return types, hiccup-only, CLJS-only, React context, goog-define, Malli). Target: 000-Vision §The pattern.

3. **Concrete CLJS choices table** (reorient §Concrete CLJS choices lines 175-199). 17-row table mapping pattern primitive → CLJS reference choice. Target: 000-Vision §Reference implementation.

4. **AI-first principles in practice** (reorient §What 'AI-first' should mean in practice lines 201-295). 11 sub-principles. The 5-layer dampening essay ('A simple dynamic model — the layered constraint') and the data-is-code-VM essay are substantive content that's referenced from AI-Audit.md and the human guide but only fully written in reorient. Target: a new Principles.md or fold into AI-Audit.md.

5. **What we retain from re-frame** list (reorient §What we retain from re-frame lines 363-377). Captures simple-dynamic-model, data-oriented-design, event-driven logic, effect maps, derived reads, single-app ergonomics, upgradeability. Target: 000-Vision.

6. **Working design implications** list (reorient §Working design implications lines 393-408). 12 design hints. Some already in Spec docs; others (definitions-serialisable, host-adapters, contracts-additive) belong in 000.

7. **Tensions and resolutions** (reorient §Tensions and resolutions lines 335-361). Some tensions resolved and captured in Spec docs (override seam, naming, frame vocab); the backwards-compat-vs-AI-first stance is in memory but not yet in any Spec doc as text. Target: 000-Vision §C1 expanded.

8. **Rationale table** (reorient §Rationale lines 51-65) pointing at on-dynamics, data-oriented-design, etc. Target: README's reading-order already covers these but the full table doesn't live anywhere else.

Once these all have permanent homes, reorient.md can be deleted (or kept as a 'historical orientation note' with a top banner pointing at the new locations).
close_reason:
reorient.md content fully migrated and the file deleted. New permanent homes:
- Identity primitive properties + per-host realisations → 000-Vision §The identity primitive (with anchor #the-identity-primitive--required-properties)
- What the pattern should NOT over-commit to → 000-Vision §What the pattern does NOT over-commit to
- Concrete CLJS choices table → 000-Vision §The reference implementation (full table)
- AI-first practical principles incl. 5-layer dampening + data-is-code-VM essays → new Principles.md
- What we retain from re-frame → 000-Vision §What re-frame2 retains from re-frame
- Working design implications → 000-Vision §Working design implications
- Tensions and resolutions → 000-Vision §Tensions and resolutions
- Rationale table → README §Rationale (also referenced from 000)
- Construction prompts as a deliverable → Principles.md §Construction prompts as a deliverable

Cross-references repointed via sed: [reorient.md §X](reorient.md...) → appropriate new home; banner phrases like 'Reframed per reorient.md' → 'Per the reorientation' (history-preserving). Examples (routing/core.cljs, ssr/core.cljc) updated. Guide had no reorient refs. Final grep confirms zero residual reorient.md references in docs/, examples/, or README.

title: Documentation voice: human is marketing/philosophy, AI is dry/precise
description:
Per Mike's directive on the two-track docs (rf2-doc-split):

HUMAN VERSION — marketing-oriented:
- Explains the philosophy and the reasons behind design choices
- Communicates the benefits (why this pattern, what it gives you)
- Includes enough technical detail to be useful, but not exhaustive
- Inherits re-frame's existing voice — opinionated, conversational, sometimes philosophical
- Read by humans evaluating, adopting, or building with re-frame2

AI VERSION — dry and precise:
- No narrative, no rationale, no marketing
- Just contracts, signatures, shapes, semantics, fixtures, prompts
- Optimised for AI consumption: read-and-act
- Aligns with the existing Construction-Prompts / Spec-Schemas / MIGRATION / conformance artefacts which are already in this register

The two are not redundant — they cover the same design but at different abstraction levels and for different consumers. A human reads the human track to understand WHY; an AI reads the AI track to know WHAT and HOW PRECISELY.
close_reason: Voice direction captured in memory rf2-doc-voice and now demonstrated across 8 chapters in docs/guide/. Three voice registers used: argumentative/philosophical (01, 08, with Perlis/Dijkstra epigraphs); code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06); migration narrative (07). Voice inherits re-frame's existing tone — opinionated, conversational, sometimes rhetorical. The AI track (docs/specification/) remains in the dry/precise register it was already in, so the contrast is clear.

title: Design two-track doc split — for-humans / for-AIs
description: Per Mike's directive: re-frame2 docs should be split into two parallel tracks. Human track: tutorials, conceptual guides, design rationale, narrative — written in re-frame's existing voice. AI track: precise contracts, construction prompts, schemas, conformance corpus, migration rules — already largely exists as separate artefacts (Construction-Prompts, Spec-Schemas, MIGRATION, conformance/). Several load-bearing questions before restructuring (see comments).
notes: Five chapters + README done. ~1280 lines: 01 (why, marketing), 02 (counter walkthrough), 03 (events/effects), 04 (views/frames), 05 (state machines), 08 (dynamic-model essay). Three voice registers on display. Remaining: 06 (server side), 07 (from re-frame v1). Stopping here for Mike to react before grinding through the last two.
close_reason: Human guide first pass complete. docs/guide/ has README plus 7 chapters (~1820 lines): 01 (why, marketing argument), 02 (counter walkthrough, code-led), 03 (events/effects, side-effects-as-data), 04 (views/frames, multi-frame), 05 (state machines, xstate adoption), 06 (server side, SSR/hydration), 07 (from re-frame v1, migration narrative), 08 (the dynamic-model essay, Dijkstra/Perlis epigraphs). Three voice registers: argumentative (01, 08), code-led narrative (02, 03, 04, 05, 06), migration-narrative (07). Two-track structure now demonstrated end-to-end: docs/guide/ for humans (this), docs/specification/ for AIs (existing). Voice consistent with re-frame's existing on-dynamics.md / data-oriented-design.md tone — opinionated, conversational, philosophical. Awaiting Mike's react before any deeper revisions.

title: Tests: missing state-machine conformance fixtures (cross-actor-send, spawn-from-action, destroy-clears-snapshot, spawn-on-spawn-callback, machine-actor-isolation)
description:
**Why:** Spec 005 §Capability matrix references fixtures that should exist for the v1 CLJS reference's claimed capabilities, but several are missing on disk.

**Missing fixtures:**

- `cross-actor-send.edn` — claimed under §Cross-actor send via `:fx`. Two registered machines; one's action dispatches an event addressed to the other; verify the receiver's snapshot transitions.
- `spawn-from-action.edn` — claimed under §Imperative spawn / destroy. An action returns `[:spawn machine-id]`; verify a child machine is created and its snapshot lives under `[:rf/machines child-id]`.
- `destroy-clears-snapshot.edn` — claimed under §Imperative spawn / destroy. After `[:destroy-machine actor-id]`, verify `[:rf/machines actor-id]` is removed from app-db.
- `spawn-on-spawn-callback.edn` — claimed under §Imperative spawn / destroy. The `:on-spawn` callback fires after spawn, with the new actor-id available.
- `machine-actor-isolation.edn` — claimed under §Own state + message ports. Two machines coexist; mutations to one's snapshot do not bleed into the other's.

**Deliverable:** five fixture EDN files plus their integration into `conformance_test.clj` (which auto-loads everything in `fixtures/`). Each fixture follows the shape of the existing `machine-transition.edn` and `invoke-spawn-on-entry-destroy-on-exit.edn`.

**Verification:** all 43+5 = 48 fixtures pass JVM conformance.

**Discovered-from:** rf2-hbt1.

title: Tests: JVM equivalents for sub-topology glitch-freedom (diamond, chain, value-equal)
description:
**Why:** the new sub-topology tests (`sub-topology-glitch-free-diamond`, `-chain`, `sub-correctness-on-value-equal-input`) are CLJS-only because they exercise Reagent's reaction propagation. The JVM reference uses the plain-atom adapter, which has different semantics (no caching, recompute every deref) but should satisfy the same glitch-free contract.

**Deliverable:** equivalent JVM tests in `implementation/test/re_frame/smoke_test.clj` exercising:
1. Diamond: app-db → {a,b} → c via compute-sub. Single mutation, c is correct, no opportunity for glitch (plain-atom recomputes each deref).
2. Chain: app-db → a → b → c via compute-sub. Same shape; verify final value.
3. Value-equal mutation: a no-op `(assoc db :unrelated "z")` keeps downstream computed values correct.

**Note:** plain-atom is glitch-impossible by construction (no caching, no propagation queue). The JVM tests are a contract-shape pin: they verify compute-sub on diamond and chain shapes returns the right algebraic result. They should NOT use Reagent.

**Discovered-from:** rf2-hbt1.

title: Test infrastructure: browser-environment runner for CLJS tests
description:
**Why:** CLJS tests currently run only via `shadow-cljs :node-test` build under Node. Real browser semantics — DOM, React's batched rendering, requestAnimationFrame, real Reagent reaction propagation timing — are not exercised.

**Deliverable:** a `shadow-cljs :browser-test` build (or equivalent Playwright / Karma harness) that runs every existing CLJS test in a headless browser and reports pass/fail.

**Scope:**
- Add the build target.
- Add a CI job that invokes it (alongside the existing `cljs` job in `.github/workflows/test.yml`).
- Verify the existing CLJS smoke + topology + nine-states tests pass in-browser.
- Document the new run command in `implementation/README.md`.

**Note:** the user already has working-tree drafts (`examples/playwright-inspect.cjs`, `examples/.edge-cdp/`, `examples/counter-dom.html`) — coordinate with them before adding new tooling, may be partial overlap.

title: State-machine API surface: docs/guide/05 references symbols not exported by re-frame-2.core
description:
docs/guide/05-state-machines.md uses these symbols, none of which are exported by `re-frame-2.core`:

- `(rf/sub-machine :auth.login/event-handler)` — sugar over a framework-shipped `:rf/machine` sub. Not in core.
- `(rf/machine-handler [:auth :login] login-flow)` — claimed helper that returns a `reg-event-fx`-shaped handler. Core exposes `create-machine-handler` instead, with a different signature (no `:machine-path`).
- `(rf/reg-machine-guard :auth.login/under-retry-limit ...)` — guard registry. Not in core.
- `(rf/reg-machine-action :auth.login/issue-request ...)` — action registry. Not in core.
- xstate-flavoured `:guards`/`:actions`/`:meta` slots inside a transition table — Spec 005's transition-table grammar may not include all of these.

Plus prose claims that may or may not match Spec 005:
- `:machine-path` as a registration metadata key for the snapshot location.
- "Transition tables are pure data, runtime owns the snapshot, headless test via `machine-transition`" — the impl matches this for the basic case, but the rich grammar in the chapter (`:cond`, `:entry`, multi-target arrays) needs verification against the transition spec.

Two questions to resolve:
1. Which of these symbols are aspirational (post-v1) vs missed exports?
2. For each one we keep aspirational, the guide chapter needs a "the API is sketched here; not all of it ships in v1" caveat — currently it reads as fully implemented.

This is the largest gap in the guide-vs-impl audit. Lower urgency than ch.02/04 because users hitting state machines will have already read enough of the spec to spot the gap.
notes:
## Decisions taken (2026-05-08)

**:data is canonical** (Spec 005 §Naming). The extended-state slot is `:data`; snapshot is `{:state :data}`. `:context` is FORBIDDEN — overloaded with re-frame's interceptor-context and React-context idioms.

**Guards/actions/sub-machine sub fns receive the whole snapshot.** They never receive `:data` directly; they receive `{:state :data :meta?}` and pull what they need.

**`sub-machine` is approved as a core convenience.** Thin wrapper over `(get-in db [:rf/machines machine-id])`. Adds one symbol to core; saves users from spelling the reserved path themselves and reinforces "the snapshot is the state".

## Open question added to scope: parameter-style standardisation

Today the corpus uses two forms inconsistently for guard/action/sub fns:

  ;; Form A (destructured) — Spec 005 §Registration L386
  (fn [{:keys [data]} _] ...)

  ;; Form B (whole-arg) — Pattern-LongRunningWork, machines.cljc impl
  (fn [snapshot _] (... (:data snapshot)))

Three plausible policies:
1. Canonical Form A everywhere.
2. Canonical Form B everywhere.
3. Both first-class — spec adds one paragraph saying either is fine.

Decision needed before ch.05 rewrite lands; otherwise the new examples will themselves drift from whatever the rest of the corpus does.

## Resulting work scope

1. ch.05 rewrite per the original points (drop reg-machine-{guard,action}, move guards/actions inside the spec map, replace machine-handler with create-machine-handler, drop :machine-path, replace :context with :data).
2. Audit and harmonise param style across Spec 005, Pattern-* docs, machines.cljc per the policy chosen above.
3. Add sub-machine to re-frame.core (one-line thin wrapper).
4. Verify the rewritten ch.05 example actually loads (smoke test or compile check).
Decision on parameter style: canonical Form A (destructure {:keys [data]}). Spec 005 examples already use this; Pattern-LongRunningWork drift to be cleaned up in the harmonisation pass. Form B (whole-arg snapshot) is the deeper contract question — filed as rf2-qppo and parked.

title: Decide frame-provider public API: rf/frame-provider Reagent component vs views/build-frame-provider factory
description:
The guide (docs/guide/04 §frame-provider, §A small example: split counter) writes:

    [rf/frame-provider {:frame :left}  [counter-view]]
    [rf/frame-provider {:frame :right} [counter-view]]

The implementation has no `rf/frame-provider` symbol. The real counter example uses a factory + frame-id positional:

    [(v/build-frame-provider :counter)
     :counter
     [counter-buttons]]

Two paths:
1. **Add `rf/frame-provider` as the documented API.** It would be a Reagent component accepting a props map `{:frame frame-id}` and children. Build it on top of `build-frame-provider` so the existing impl substrate is preserved. Wins: the prose-shape (`{:frame :left}`) reads idiomatically and matches React-context conventions; one symbol to import.
2. **Update the guide to use `views/build-frame-provider`.** Cost: clunkier idiom (`[(v/build-frame-provider :left) :left [counter]]`); duplicates the frame id; requires an extra `:as v` import.

Path 1 looks like the natural shipping API; the guide may have been the source of truth and the impl drifted. Decision drives whether the guide gets edited or the impl grows a thin wrapper.

title: Decide reg-view body: auto-inject dispatch/subscribe vs require dispatcher/subscriber capture
description:
The guide (docs/guide/02 §The view, docs/guide/04 §The Var-reference idiom) states that inside a `reg-view` body, `dispatch` and `subscribe` are *injected* — bound to the surrounding frame — so view bodies can be frame-blind:

    (rf/reg-view :counter
      (fn []
        [:div
         [:button {:on-click #(dispatch [:counter/inc])} "+"]
         [:span @(subscribe [:count])]]))

The implementation does NOT inject them. The real example in `examples/counter/core.cljs` requires explicit capture:

    (reg-view :counter-buttons
      (fn []
        (let [d (rf/dispatcher)
              s (rf/subscriber)]
          [:div
           [:button {:on-click #(d [:counter/dec])} "-"]
           [:span @(s [:count])]])))

Two paths:
1. **Make the impl match the guide** — the macro `reg-view` rewrites the body to bind locals `dispatch`/`subscribe` to frame-bound fns captured at render time. Cost: macro complexity + potential aliasing surprises if the user already has those names in scope. Win: the prose claim becomes true and view bodies are visibly frame-blind.
2. **Make the guide match the impl** — document the `(rf/dispatcher)` / `(rf/subscriber)` capture pattern as canonical. Cost: every view body now opens with two `let` bindings; less elegant. Win: zero macro magic.

Decision needed before mechanical doc fixes can complete the namespace sweep — without a direction, every view example in the guide either over-promises or under-explains.

title: Spec: should normative requirements be rewritten in RFC 2119 phrasing (MUST / SHOULD / MAY)?
description:
Question raised during the implementation work: would the spec corpus (docs/specification/*.md) read more cleanly — and produce fewer 'is this mandatory or just descriptive?' beads — if the normative language followed RFC 2119 conventions (MUST / SHOULD / MAY etc.)?

Motivation:
- Several beads filed this session (rf2-r044, rf2-smba, rf2-sklf, rf2-pz9f) exist because the current spec phrasing doesn't separate 'this is the contract' from 'this is what the reference impl does'. Normative keywords force that separation.
- AI implementers (the spec's stated audience per Goal 2) parse MUST / SHOULD reliably; conformance fixtures could be mechanically cross-referenced, since each fixture pins a specific MUST.
- Cross-runtime portability — host language ports targeting the same spec need the same MUST / MAY split.

Counter-arguments:
- The current spec has a distinctive narrative voice. Wholesale RFC-2119-ification would flatten it. The 'spec-as-argument' function (000-Vision arguing why re-frame2 exists) doesn't suit normative phrasing.
- Cost: ~14 spec docs to rewrite, all cross-references break.

Suggested middle path (cheapest experiment first):
- Pick ONE doc — 002-Frames.md is the most heavily-referenced contract.
- Add a 'Contract' / 'Normative requirements' block near the top with MUST / SHOULD / MAY bullets, each cross-referenced to its conformance fixture(s).
- Leave the rest of the doc in narrative voice for rationale / examples.
- See if it earns its keep — easier to evolve, mechanically grep-able for fixture mapping, doesn't lose readability.
- If yes, scale to other docs; if it clutters more than helps, abandon.

Either way, this is a Mike-judgement call. Filed for explicit consideration rather than as an action item.

title: reg-view macro: outer (def x ...) wrapper double-defs against the macro's own def
description:
Across the v2-API-aligned examples (login/, realworld/*, routing/, 7guis/, ssr/) every reg-view call follows the pattern:

  (def login-form
    (reg-view :auth.login/form
      {:doc "..."}
      (fn render-auth-login-form [] ...)))

The reg-view macro (re-frame-2.views-macros) expands to
  (def <kw-name> (reg-view* <kw> <meta> <fn>))
i.e. (def form (reg-view* :auth.login/form ...)) — which evaluates to the var #'form.

The outer (def login-form ...) then binds login-form to that var, producing a var-of-var. Reagent's auto-deref MAY rescue this in practice but it's brittle and Spec 004 §reg-view defs the Var by default already commits to the macro defining the Var with the keyword's name.

Pick one resolution:
(a) Drop the outer (def some-name ...) wrappers across the examples and reference [form] / [banner] / [root] / [preview] / [home] / etc. directly — the macro already binds them.
(b) Change the macro so reg-view does NOT auto-def — let users wrap in their own (def some-name (reg-view* ...)). Spec 004 §reg-view defs the Var would need to relax.
(c) Extend the macro to accept an optional :as some-name so users pick the local name.

Implementation today follows (a) per Spec 004; the recently-landed example commit landed (b)-style wrappers without removing the macro's auto-def. Pick a direction and align spec, macro, and examples in one pass.
notes:
Empirically: in CLJS the double-def works because (def x y) at the CLJS level evaluates y and returns the SAME JS value (no Var-objects like JVM Clojure). So (def login-form (reg-view :auth/form ...)) results in BOTH login-form AND form bound to the same MetaFn — two names, same value. Verified via reg-view-macro-double-def-investigation in runtime-cljs-test (commit pending).

The pattern works at runtime but is still:
- Two names for the same view (semantic duplication / drift risk).
- Spec 004 says reg-view defs the Var by-keyword-name (so 'login-form' shouldn't exist; only 'form' should).
- Reliant on CLJS-specific def-returns-value semantics; the same pattern in JVM-side code would create var-of-var.

Reduces severity from 'breaks at runtime' to 'spec-vs-example mismatch worth aligning when convenient'. Pick a resolution per the bead body.

title: Spec: 013-Flows must reflect frame-scoped registration / clear semantics
description:
Spec 013 currently treats flows as a process-global registry — reg-flow and clear-flow take only a flow id; the description in §Drain integration / §Reference doesn't say which frame they belong to. The implementation has now moved to frame-scoped flows (commit 86ade3a):

  (rf/reg-flow flow)              ; defaults frame to (current-frame)
  (rf/reg-flow flow {:frame :f})  ; explicit
  (rf/clear-flow id)
  (rf/clear-flow id {:frame :f})

Update Spec 013 to:
- State explicitly that flows are frame-scoped (per the rf2-jflj rationale: undo / time-travel boundaries are frame-local).
- Document the :frame opt on reg-flow / clear-flow.
- Update §Drain integration to clarify run-flows! evaluates only the called frame's flows.
- Update §Reference to show the registry shape: {frame-id {flow-id flow-map}}.
- Re-affirm that the :rf.fx/reg-flow / :rf.fx/clear-flow runtime fx receive {:frame frame-id} from the cofx and thread it through.

title: Spec: h macro view-vs-DOM-tag heuristic is undocumented
description:
Spec 004 §The h macro says h walks hiccup at compile time and rewrites [:keyword args] into runtime view-resolution calls 'for every keyword present in the view registry'. But:
