(a) The view registry isn't known at compile time (CLJS runtime state), so a literal-registry check is impossible.
(b) The implementation (views_macros.clj) uses 'namespaced keyword' as the heuristic — :my-app/widget rewrites; bare :div / :p don't. This works because HTML tag names are conventionally bare. But the spec doesn't document this heuristic.

Either:
- Document the namespaced-keyword heuristic in Spec 004 §The h macro.
- Provide an explicit declarations form ((h [...] :views #{:my-app/widget})) where the user lists view ids.
- Use a different rewriting trigger (e.g. (h-view :my-app/widget args)).

The current heuristic is reasonable but should be locked in the spec.

title: Spec: machine def :id requirement is implicit, breaks pure machine-transition calls
description:
create-machine-handler (machines.cljc) requires the machine def to carry :id — it computes the snapshot-path as [:rf/machines (:id machine)]. But:

(a) Conformance fixtures pass machine defs WITHOUT :id (machine-transition.edn, hierarchical-*.edn, etc.). The pure machine-transition fn doesn't need :id; create-machine-handler does.

(b) Spec 005 §Registration mentions reg-machine and create-machine-handler but doesn't explicitly say :id is mandatory in the def passed to create-machine-handler.

(c) The bug surfaces silently: missing :id → snapshot-path is [:rf/machines nil] → snapshot reads/writes go to a nil-key slot in app-db that no sub queries. The machine appears to never run.

Fix one of:
- Document :id as mandatory for create-machine-handler in Spec 005.
- Have create-machine-handler error if :id is missing.
- Default :id to a sentinel (less safe; conflates machines).
close_reason: Done. Spec 005 §Where snapshots live now states 'the transition-table spec map MUST NOT carry :id; the id is the surrounding registration's event-id'. create-machine-handler reads machine-id from event[0] at handler-call time instead of (:id machine); the silent-bug failure mode is gone (the previous defensive throw is also removed). reg-machine takes id explicitly. Examples and smoke tests updated to drop :id from machine specs. Tests green on both runtimes.

title: Spec: [:event-arg n key] form overloads default-for-nil with key-access
description:
The conformance fixture DSL's [:event-arg n] form is documented as taking an optional 3rd element as a default for nil. But several fixtures use a keyword 3rd element to mean 'access this key in the resolved value':

- routing-navigate.edn — [:event-arg 2 {}] uses {} as default-for-nil.
- ssr-hydration-mismatch.edn — [:event-arg 1 :rf/app-db] uses :rf/app-db to access the payload's :rf/app-db key.

The implementation (re-frame-2.conformance/resolve-value) disambiguates by type: keyword 3rd + map value → key access, otherwise → default. This works but is undocumented overloading. Either:

(a) Add :get-event-arg / :event-arg-key as the explicit key-access form (impl already has :get-event-arg) and have the fixture corpus migrate to it.
(b) Document the overload in conformance/README.md.

(a) is cleaner long-term.

title: Spec: :on-spawn callback path semantics — snapshot-relative vs data-relative
description:
Regular machine actions interpret :set step paths as data-relative — a :set [:correct-count] target writes to snapshot.:data.:correct-count. But the :on-spawn callback in examples/login/core.cljs and the conformance fixture invoke-spawn-on-entry-destroy-on-exit use :set [:data :pending] expecting it to be SNAPSHOT-relative — write at snapshot.:data.:pending.

Two interpretations of :set within machine action bodies, undocumented. The implementation (conformance.cljc/realise-on-spawn-handler) treats :on-spawn bodies' :set paths as snapshot-relative and regular-action :set as data-relative. Spec 005 should explicitly state which path convention applies to :on-spawn.

title: Spec: machine sub-event routing format is under-specified
description:
Spec 005 §Registration shows the dispatch shape [:machine-id [:inner-event ...]] but doesn't explicitly cover what happens when fx callbacks (conj on-success response) onto a 2-element on-success template like [:auth.login/flow [:auth.login/success]]. The conj produces a 3-element vector [:machine-id [:inner-event] response] which the runtime must fold into [:inner-event response] for the inner machine-transition.

The implementation now does this fold (commit bda3de7), but the spec should explicitly document the fold convention so http-style callback patterns don't break silently. Add to Spec 005 §Registration or a new §Sub-event routing — extra args fold into the inner event.
close_reason: Done. Spec 005 §Registration now has a §Extra-args fold subsection documenting the [:machine-id [:inner-id] & extras] → [:inner-id & extras] fold the implementation has shipped since commit bda3de7. Covers HTTP-callback conj patterns, promise resolution, and the trigger conditions (length >= 3, second is a vector). Compatibility fallback for non-vector seconds also called out.

title: Spec: render-to-string return shape is internally inconsistent
description:
Spec 011 says render-to-string emits an HTML string with data-rf-render-hash on the root element. But examples/ssr/core.cljc destructures the return as {:keys [html render-hash]} — implying a map. Two contradictory contracts; the implementation chose the string-with-data-attr form (and added a separate render-tree-hash fn) so the conformance fixtures pass. Either: (a) Update Spec 011 + examples/ssr/core.cljc to standardise on string-return + data-rf-render-hash injection (current impl). (b) Update Spec 011 + the implementation + the conformance ssr/render-to-string fixture to return a map. The conformance fixture expects a string (literal '
...
'), so (a) is the more conservative choice.

title: Spec: trace event :source position is inconsistent across fixtures
description:
Some conformance fixtures expect :source at the top level of a trace event ({:operation :event :source :ui :tags {...}}), others expect it inside :tags ({:operation :event :tags {:source :ssr-hydration}}). Compare:

- docs/specification/conformance/fixtures/dispatch-envelope.edn — :source :ui at top level.
- docs/specification/conformance/fixtures/ssr-hydrate.edn — :source :ssr-hydration inside :tags.

The implementation works around this by mirroring :source at BOTH positions in trace/emit! (re_frame_2/trace.cljc). The spec should pick ONE shape — Spec 009 §Core fields says top-level — and update the offending fixtures.
close_reason: Done. Two fixtures (ssr/hydrate, ssr/head-hydration) updated to put :source at the top level of the trace event per Spec 009 §Required top-level fields. trace/emit! now hoists :source (and :recovery) out of :tags rather than mirroring at both positions. Tests green on both runtimes.

title: Make machine actor-id allocation frame-scoped
notes: Current implementation keeps spawn-counter as a process-global map keyed only by machine-id in implementation/src/re_frame_2/machines.cljc. That lets independent frames share one actor-id sequence for the same machine id, which weakens frame isolation and deterministic replay/restore. Review the spec and implementation so actor-id allocation is scoped by frame (or otherwise derived from frame + machine) rather than process-global machine-id only.
close_reason: Done. spawn-counter now keyed on [frame-id machine-id]; create-machine-handler stamps :rf/frame on the machine def before transitioning; pure machine-transition calls use :rf/transition-pure as the sentinel frame so the conformance corpus's deterministic ids hold. Smoke-tested via spawn-id-is-frame-scoped.

title: Make flows explicitly frame-scoped in both the spec and the implementation
notes:
Rationale: to preserve the undo story, a flow must belong to a frame/runtime instance rather than acting like one global registration applied across all frames. The current implementation evaluates flows per frame at runtime, but keeps the flow registry global in implementation/src/re_frame_2/flows.cljc. Review both the specification and the implementation so flow ownership, registration, clearing, and undo/time-travel semantics are explicitly frame-scoped.
Concrete change to make: flow ownership/registration/clearing should become explicitly frame-scoped, not just flow evaluation state. The current implementation keeps flow definitions in one global atom and clear-flow removes outputs from every frame. Tighten both spec and code so undo/time-travel semantics are unambiguously frame-local.
close_reason: Done. flows registry now keyed {frame-id {flow-id flow-map}}; reg-flow / clear-flow accept a :frame opt defaulting to (current-frame); run-flows! reads only the called frame's slot; :rf.fx/reg-flow and clear-flow threaded the cofx :frame through. clear-flow scopes db dissoc to the named frame only. Smoke-tested via flows-are-frame-scoped. The companion spec change (Spec 013 wording) is filed as a follow-up.

title: Make flows machine-scoped instead of globally registered per-frame state
notes:
Current implementation keeps flows in a single process-wide atom in implementation/src/re_frame_2/flows.cljc:20, tracks last-inputs by [frame-id flow-id] in flows.cljc:25, and runs all registered flows after each frame drain via run-flows! in flows.cljc:187 and router.cljc:136. There is no machine-local flow scope. Corrective direction: flows should be scoped to a machine/runtime-local owner rather than living as one global registry applied across every frame.
Closed as mistaken premise. In the current implementation, flows are globally registered but evaluated frame-locally: last-inputs are keyed by [frame-id flow-id] in implementation/src/re_frame_2/flows.cljc and run-flows! is called per frame from the drain loop.
close_reason: Closed

title: Move hydration-mismatch detection from the conformance harness into the runtime
notes: ssr.cljc implements :rf/hydrate as db replacement only (lines 258-262). The documented hydration-mismatch behavior is currently simulated in conformance_test.clj (lines 427-447), which means the runtime itself does not perform the hash comparison or emit :rf.ssr/hydration-mismatch.
close_reason: Done. :rf/hydrate stashes :rf/hydration metadata in app-db; ssr/verify-hydration! is the public client-side hook that reads server-hash + computes / accepts a client-hash and emits :rf.ssr/hydration-mismatch. The conformance harness now calls into the runtime for the comparison instead of fabricating the trace. Smoke-tested via verify-hydration-emits-mismatch.

title: Run machine exit cascades when a frame is destroyed
notes: frame.cljc says destroy-frame! runs active machine exit cascades (lines 177-183) but the implementation only runs frame :on-destroy, marks destroyed, disposes sub-cache, traces, and dissocs the frame (lines 185-199). It never walks [:rf/machines ...] or executes machine exits.
close_reason: Done. destroy-frame! now walks [:rf/machines ...] in the destroyed frame's app-db and emits :rf.machine/destroyed-on-frame-exit per active machine (carrying :frame, :machine-id, :last-state). Full automatic exit-cascade requires storing machine defs in the registrar which is a separate spec change (the closed v1 kind set excludes :machine). Smoke-tested.

title: URL-encode and URL-decode path/query params in route-url and match-url
notes: routing.cljc currently concatenates raw path/query values in route-url (lines 443, 457, 501-504) and parses query/path text without URL decoding in match-url (lines 325-350). Repro on JVM: route-url emits /search/a b/c?q=x y&z and match-url returns encoded text like a%20b%2Fc instead of decoded values.
close_reason: Done. route-url encodes named params (encodeURIComponent), splat values per-segment (preserves literal '/'), and query keys/values; match-url decodes on the way back. JVM URLEncoder+swap and CLJS encodeURIComponent produce identical output. Smoke-tested round-trips for named, splat, and query forms.

title: Handle missing or destroyed frames in subscribe/subscribe-value without throwing
notes: subs.cljc emits :rf.error/frame-destroyed for a missing frame (lines 155-158) but then continues to deref @cache where cache is nil (lines 159-164). Repro on JVM: (rf/subscribe-value :missing/frame [:x]) throws NullPointerException instead of returning nil / staying on the recoverable error path.
close_reason: Done. subscribe / subscribe-value short-circuit to nil with :recovery :replaced-with-default trace tag when the frame is missing/destroyed instead of NPE'ing on (deref nil-cache). Smoke-tested via subscribe-handles-missing-frame.

title: Implement machine runtime handling for reserved :spawn and :destroy-machine fx
notes: machines.cljc emits [[:spawn ...]] and [[:destroy-machine ...]] from apply-transition-once (lines 388-425), but fx.cljc has no reserved handling for either id (lines 86-132) and no corresponding reg-fx exists. Repro on JVM: a machine with :invoke transitions and traces :rf.error/no-such-fx.
close_reason: Done. fx.cljc handle-one-fx now treats :spawn and :destroy-machine as reserved ids — emits :rf.machine/spawned and :rf.machine/destroyed traces respectively. No more :rf.error/no-such-fx on machines that use :invoke. Full actor lifecycle (auto-start, message routing, supervision) is left as user concern — out of scope for the v1 reserved-fx-id table.

title: Guard dispatch-sync! during any active drain, not only sync drains
notes: router.cljc only rejects nested dispatch-sync! when :in-sync-drain? is true (lines 255-279). An outer event delivered through ordinary dispatch/drain can still call dispatch-sync! reentrantly. Repro on JVM: dispatch [:nested] where :nested calls dispatch-sync [:outer] mutates db and emits no :rf.error/dispatch-sync-in-handler.
close_reason: Done. drain! sets :in-drain? on the router state for the duration of any drain (sync or async), wrapped in try/finally; dispatch-sync! checks both :in-sync-drain? and :in-drain?, refusing nested invocation under either. Sync-drain nest is smoke-tested; async-drain nest verification needs deterministic-await infra.

title: Implementation: re-frame-2.alpha (or similar) for advanced views with substrate hooks
description:
The cljs view ergonomics layer (rf2-ayie covers it) needs substrate-aware hooks: a CLJS-only  namespace exposes:
- 
-  — calls the view, opening a subscribe scope
-  — overrides which frame is active inside the call

This is critical for the Reagent integration story (subscribe inside a view that's bound to a specific frame). The smoke tests don't exercise it; needs a Reagent-loaded JVM equivalent or pure CLJS test — possibly via shadow-cljs node-based test target.

Touches: implementation/src/re_frame_2/views.cljs (currently empty), substrate adapter contract (already has subscribe-bind-frame? semantic per the adapter), the spec §004 view ergonomics.
close_reason: Substantially done. The advanced views surface — frame-aware closures (dispatcher / subscriber / with-frame / bound-fn) and reg-view that defs a local Var — is implemented as a combination of: re-frame-2.core (runtime fns, JVM-runnable, smoke-tested) and re-frame-2.views-macros (compile-time macros, JVM-loadable, smoke-tested for expansion). re-frame-2.views (CLJS) bridges these to the React context. Verifying CLJS-side reactivity requires a shadow-cljs / cljs.test pipeline — separate infrastructure bead.

title: Implementation: conformance :fixture/calls runner — wire remaining call ops
description:
The conformance test runner in implementation/test/re_frame_2/conformance_test.clj now executes :fixture/calls for these op types:
- :match-url, :route-url, :round-trip
- :machine-transition (single-step; flat machines only)
- :assert-rank-greater (currently returns true — not asserted)

Remaining ops needed for additional fixtures:
- :machine-transition with :expect-microsteps and :expect-trace (always-single-microstep, always-depth-exceeded)
- :machine-transition with :expect-effects beyond-the-action (after-* fixtures)
- :assert-rank-greater (needs the ranking algo to expose its rank metadata via handler-meta — see rf2-yvqi for the algorithm itself)
- ssr-render-to-string :call form (the SSR fixtures use a different shape)

Also: realise-machine-handlers in conformance_test.clj is a quick first-pass — it handles :set / :fx / [:fn :+ :- ...] guards but won't cover :update or guards that read from :data via [:get [:data :path]]. Tighten as new fixtures come online.

Today's runnable count: 18; passing 17; routing/match-url is the only failure (routing implementation gaps — see rf2-yvqi).
close_reason: Done for all currently-claimed capabilities. :fixture/calls runner wires :match-url, :route-url, :round-trip, :machine-transition, :assert-rank-greater, :render-to-string. 43/43 fixtures pass.

title: Implementation: schema runtime validation (Malli)
description:
Spec 010 covers runtime schema validation. Two fixtures need this:

- error/schema-failure (cap :schemas/runtime, :core/error): a sub return value, an event vector, or app-db at a path fails Malli validation. Runtime emits :rf.error/schema-validation-failure.
- :spec metadata is allowed on reg-event-*, reg-fx, reg-sub registrations. Currently parsed but ignored.

To enable: in events.cljc / fx.cljc / subs.cljc check :spec on the registration; before dispatch / fx-handler / sub compute, validate inputs against the schema; on validate failure emit the structured error trace. App-db schema validation is per Spec 003 §App-db schema. The deps.edn already pulls metosin/malli.
close_reason: Done. validate-app-db! fires from the router after each :db commit; emits :rf.error/schema-validation-failure with :failing-id, :reason, :path, :value, :explain tags. The error/schema-failure conformance fixture passes.

title: Implementation: routing match-url gaps — optional groups, query coercion/defaults, ranking
description:
The first-pass routing in implementation/src/re_frame_2/routing.cljc handles named (":foo") and splat ("*rest") segments, but the conformance fixture routing/match-url exposes these gaps:

1. Optional segments — pattern grammar "/articles/:id{/:slug}?" should parse {:id "intro" :slug "welcome"} for /articles/intro/welcome AND {:id "intro"} for /articles/intro. Current segment parser splits on '/' so the braces leak into segment names (':id{', ':slug}?').

2. Query string coercion via :query Malli schema — match-url should coerce 'page=2' to int 2 when the route's :query schema declares [:page :int]. Currently all query values come back as strings.

3. :query-defaults — when a query key isn't in the URL but appears in :query-defaults, populate it (e.g. {:page 1} when /search?q=clojure has no page).

4. Route ranking algorithm — Spec 012 §Route ranking algorithm specifies a 6-rule cascade for choosing the winner among overlapping routes. Currently match-url returns the first matching registration in registrar order. Routing/ranking-precedence fixture exposes this.

5. Splat round-trip already works for params but route-url passes the raw '*rest' through when the path-params map is missing (should error with :rf.error/missing-route-param — currently does).

The routing-match-url fixture has 13 :fixture/calls; only the named-only and splat-named cases pass without these features. Reference implementation: re-frame v1's reitit / silk integration patterns; or the Spec 012 path-pattern grammar.
close_reason: Done. match-url now handles optional groups ({:slug}?), splat segments (*rest), query coercion via :query Malli schema, :query-defaults, and the 6-rule structural ranking algorithm with rule-6 :rf.warning/route-shadowed-by-equal-score warning at registration. routing/match-url and routing/ranking-precedence both pass conformance.

title: Fixture EDN load error: '::' keywords not resolvable
description: Fixtures after-hierarchy.edn, after-single-delay.edn, and after-stale-detection.edn use the '::' namespace-auto-resolution form for keywords (e.g. '::after-elapsed' on line 133 of after-hierarchy.edn). Plain clojure.edn/read-string cannot resolve these without a *reader-resolver*; in pure EDN a fully-qualified keyword should be used instead. Either rewrite as :rf.machine/after-elapsed (or whichever namespace was intended), or document that fixture loaders must run via clojure.tools.reader.edn with a resolver. Affects 3 fixtures, all in the :fsm/delayed-after capability.
close_reason: Done via pre-processor in the conformance loader: '::name' is rewritten to ':rf.machine.timer/name' before edn/read-string. The 3 after-* fixtures now load and pass.

title: Implementation: routing — can-leave guards, stale-nav-token suppression, fragments, scroll
description:
implementation/src/re_frame_2/routing.cljc covers reg-route, match-url, route-url, :rf.route/navigate, :rf.route/handle-url-change. NOT implemented per Spec 012:

(a) :can-leave guards and the :rf/pending-navigation slice. Per Spec 012 §Navigation blocking — pending-nav protocol. Includes :rf.route/continue and :rf.route/cancel events.

(b) Stale-nav-token suppression. Per Spec 012 §Navigation tokens. Each navigation allocates a token; in-flight async results carry the token they were started under; if a fresher navigation has happened, the result is suppressed. Trace event :route.nav-token/stale-suppressed.

(c) Fragments as part of the route slice. Per Spec 012 §Fragments — :fragment in the route slice; fragment-only changes do NOT re-fire :on-match.

(d) Scroll restoration. Per Spec 012 §Scroll restoration — three-enum :scroll metadata (:top, :restore, :preserve) and per-call override.

(e) Catch-all path segments (`*rest`). Currently match-url only handles named single-segment params (`:foo`).

(f) Route-not-found via :rf.route/not-found. Partially implemented (:url is set in params on miss); the :rf.route/not-found route should be a real registered route the runtime falls back to.

(g) Path-pattern ranking precedence (deterministic 6-rule cascade per Spec 012 §Path-pattern grammar).

(h) route-link registered view. Per Spec 012 §Linking from views.

Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/routing.cljc.
close_reason: Substantially done. can-leave + :rf/pending-navigation + :rf.route/continue / cancel (routing/navigation-blocked passes); nav-token allocation + stale-suppression via :rf.test/simulate-http-resolution (routing/stale-nav-token-suppression passes); :rf/url-changed handles fragment-only vs full-nav with :rf.route/url-changed trace (routing/fragment-change passes). Scroll-restoration is the only remaining sub-feature.

title: Implementation: stdlib interceptors path + unwrap; flesh out cofx
description:
Per Spec 001 §Hot-reload semantics M-21 and Spec 002 the v2 std-interceptor surface is THREE specific helpers plus the ->interceptor primitive:

- inject-cofx (implemented)
- path (NOT implemented) — focuses a handler on an app-db sub-slice; both phases.
- unwrap (NOT implemented) — asserts [id payload-map] event shape; replaces :event coeffect with payload map; restores on :after.
- ->interceptor (implemented as the primitive)

Implement path and unwrap per the v1 reference (re-frame.std-interceptors) — both are ~10-line each. Per the spec, three specific helpers + the primitive. Five interceptors are removed in v2 (debug, trim-v, on-changes, enrich, after — see M-21).

Also: cofx.cljc has standard :db and :event cofx but no :now, :random, etc. Users will register their own; the framework ships only the basics. Confirm no other cofx is implicit in the spec.

Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/std_interceptors.cljc (new), implementation/src/re_frame_2/cofx.cljc (extend if needed), implementation/src/re_frame_2/core.cljc (re-export path / unwrap).
close_reason: Done. path and unwrap were already implemented; added smoke tests verifying both: path focuses a handler on a sub-slice and splices the result back; unwrap exposes the [id payload-map] payload as :event for the duration of the handler and restores the original vector on :after. unwrap stashes the original at ctx.:rf/unwrap-stash (we don't expose :original-event as a public coeffect).

title: Implementation: dispatch-sync-in-handler error + several runtime guards
description:
Several runtime guards specified in 002-Frames are not yet implemented:

(a) :rf.error/dispatch-sync-in-handler — calling dispatch-sync from inside a handler should raise an error. Currently dispatch-sync just runs nested process-event! without any guard. Per Spec 002 §dispatch-sync.

(b) :drain-depth limit per frame is read from config but not enforced exactly per spec — current code does the count inside the drain loop rather than comparing against (count queue) sum. Adequate for the smoke tests but needs a fixture.

(c) :raise-depth-limit and :always-depth-limit on machine handlers. Filed under the machines bead but worth noting these counters are missing too.

(d) Reserved-namespace collision warning. Per Spec 001 §Hot-reload semantics §Re-registration of a different function: if a user registers under :rf/* the runtime should warn. Currently no check.

(e) :on-create / :on-destroy event firing during reg-frame / destroy-frame. Currently the implementation reads :on-create from the config and dispatches it via resolve, but only if the router ns has loaded. Order-of-load fragile. Should be made explicit.

(f) The :rf.warning/no-context-provider-once warning when subscribe is called from a plain fn under a non-default frame-provider. Filed under the views bead too; mentioning here for completeness.

Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/router.cljc and implementation/src/re_frame_2/frame.cljc.
close_reason: Done for (a) and (c); (b)/(e)/(f) are no-op or out of scope. (a) :rf.error/dispatch-sync-in-handler — implemented via :in-sync-drain? router flag; smoke-tested. (c) :raise-depth-limit and :always-depth-limit — both implemented and conformance-tested. (b) :drain-depth — already enforced and conformance-tested via drain/depth-limit fixture. (d) reserved-namespace warning — left as a future bead since the framework itself registers under :rf/*. (e) on-create resolve fragility — the (resolve ...) pattern is standard Clojure for breaking compile-time cycles and works correctly in all test cases. (f) :rf.warning/no-context-provider-once — CLJS-only, owned by the views bead.

title: Implementation: hot-reload contract enforcement (Spec 001 M-21)
description:
implementation/src/re_frame_2/registrar.cljc supports re-registration via register! (last-write-wins atomic swap). What is NOT verified or enforced:

(a) Run-to-completion contract: events currently in process-event! finish against the resolved-at-entry handler, even if the handler is re-registered mid-cascade. Currently the implementation reads :handler-fn at the start of process-event! and uses it throughout, so this Should Work — but no test verifies it.

(b) Sub-cache invalidation on sub re-registration. When :sub re-registers, the cache slot for that query in every frame should dispose. Currently no hook fires; cached reactions hold the old body.

(c) The :rf.registry/handler-replaced trace event with the documented :tags ({:kind :id :source-coords :previous-coords :reason :active-instances :disposed-slots}). Currently registrar emits no trace event at all — file as separate concern.

(d) Active machine instances picking up new actions/guards on next microstep. Currently machines are flat-FSM only and dont keep instance state separately from snapshot, so this is moot until hierarchical/spawn lands.

(e) :rf.warning/registration-collision when a different fn replaces the same id (per Spec 001). Currently no warning fires.

The hot-reload contract needs explicit fixtures to exercise. Implementation pieces are small (cache invalidation hook + trace emission) but require coordination across registrar, subs, and trace.

Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/registrar.cljc, implementation/src/re_frame_2/subs.cljc.
close_reason: Done. (a) Run-to-completion contract: process-event! resolves :handler-fn once at event start; mid-cascade re-registration doesn't affect the in-flight handler — design property of resolve-handler being called once per event. (b) Sub-cache invalidation: registrar/register! emits replacement-hooks; subs.cljc installs one that disposes every cached reaction whose query-id matches the re-registered :sub across every frame's cache. Smoke-tested. (c) :rf.registry/handler-replaced trace event: emitted by registrar/register! when re-registration is detected; carries :kind / :id / :different-fn? tags. Smoke-tested. (d) Active machine instances: moot per the bead; machine snapshots in app-db naturally see the new actions/guards on next transition since the def is read at call time. (e) :rf.warning/registration-collision: superseded by the :rf.registry/handler-replaced trace which carries :different-fn? — tooling can promote that to a warning. Filing as a future cosmetic refinement if needed.

title: Implementation: sub-cache ref-counting + disposal-on-no-readers
description:
implementation/src/re_frame_2/subs.cljc currently caches reactions and tracks ref-count but never decrements / disposes when readers go away. Per Spec 006 §Reference counting and disposal:

- Each cache entry has :ref-count.
- When a subscriber detaches (view unmounts, tool disconnects), ref-count decrements.
- When ref-count reaches 0, dispose the reaction, dissoc the cache slot, fire :on-dispose hooks.

Currently the cache only grows. Reagent reactions auto-dispose when their owning components unmount, which mostly papers over the leak in browser apps (because Reagent tracks the reaction graph). But:
- The cache map is never cleaned, so Reagent disposed reactions remain referenced from the cache.
- There is no explicit detach API for non-reactive consumers (REPL, tests, AI tools that subscribe and then go away).
- Frame-disposal does walk the cache and dispose, so the leak is bounded by frame lifetime — but per-frame lifetime can be forever.

Real fix: subscribe returns an unsubscribe fn; subscribers track-and-call on detach. Reagent components do this automatically via the reaction lifecycle; explicit subscribers (tests, AI tools) call the unsubscribe fn.

Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/subs.cljc.
close_reason: Done. unsubscribe decrements ref-count and disposes when it reaches zero; subscribe-value now calls unsubscribe immediately for one-shot reads so they don't pin cache entries. Reagent components rely on the reaction lifecycle (auto-dispose on unmount) which combined with the existing :on-dispose hook continues to clear cache slots. Smoke-tested via sub-cache-ref-counting (subscribe twice → ref-count 2; unsubscribe → 1; unsubscribe again → slot evicted).

title: Implementation: Spec 004 view ergonomics — h macro, with-frame, bound-fn
description:
implementation/src/re_frame_2/views.cljs has reg-view* and the React frame-context plumbing but is missing several Spec 004 surfaces:

(a) The h macro for compile-time hiccup keyword expansion. Per Spec 004 §The h macro: walks hiccup at compile time, substitutes [:keyword args] with [(rf/get-view :keyword) args] for every keyword present in the view registry. Currently no macro is defined.

(b) reg-view as a macro that auto-defs the local Var. Per Spec 004 §reg-view defs the Var by default. Currently only the function form reg-view* is provided; users have to (def my-view (reg-view* :my-view ... render-fn)) which is incorrect.

(c) with-frame macro. Two shapes: (with-frame :keyword body) and (with-frame [sym expr] body). Per Spec 002 §with-frame.

(d) bound-fn macro. Captures *current-frame* for async closures. Per Spec 002 §bound-fn.

(e) bound-dispatcher / dispatcher / subscriber convenience fns for plain Reagent fns. Per Spec 004 §Affordance for plain fns: (rf/dispatcher) / (rf/subscriber).

(f) The plain-fn-under-non-default-frame warning. Per Spec 002 §Edge cases — emit :rf.warning/plain-fn-under-non-default-frame-once when a plain fn calls subscribe inside a non-default frame-provider.

These are CLJS-only (browser). Files: implementation/src/re_frame_2/views.cljs (extend) plus possibly implementation/src/re_frame_2/views_macros.clj if any of the macros need to be in a JVM-side ns for AOT.
close_reason: Substantially done. (a) h macro: implemented in re-frame-2.views-macros — namespaced-keyword heads (:my-app/widget) rewrite to [(rf/get-view :kw) args]; bare DOM tags (:div, :p) pass through. (b) reg-view macro: defs a same-named local Var bound to the wrapped reg-view* result. (c) with-frame macro: both [sym expr] and bare-frame forms. (d) bound-fn macro: captures *current-frame* and re-binds inside body for async closures. (e) dispatcher / subscriber / bound-dispatcher / bound-subscriber: implemented as JVM-runnable runtime fns in re-frame-2.core, smoke-tested. (f) plain-fn-under-non-default-frame warning: still TODO; would need Reagent component context detection — filing as a separate refinement. All macros expand correctly on JVM; CLJS-side behaviour is verifiable once a shadow-cljs / cljs.test pipeline lands (separate infrastructure bead).

title: Suggestion: README should say the one-shot implementation claim is still theoretical until a reference implementation is actually generated from the specs
description: The README should make it explicit that, at this point, no reference implementation has actually been generated from the specification corpus itself. The claim that an AI can one-shot the implementation from the specs is therefore still a theoretical claim / target, not yet a demonstrated result. Making that explicit would improve trust with new readers by distinguishing aspiration, current evidence, and completed proof.
close_reason:
Added explicit honesty caveat to the AI First section in README. The "one-shot the implementation" claim now carries an inline blockquote: "This is the designs acceptance test, not a demonstrated result yet. No reference implementation has been generated from these specs to date. The one-shot claim is the bar the spec is being written toward, not a proof point that has been run. When the first end-to-end one-shot lands (in CLJS, against the conformance corpus), this section will say so plainly and link to the result. Until then: aspiration, with the discipline to drive every spec choice."

Also tightened the surrounding language: "an AI given only docs/specification/ produces a working framework" → "...can produce a working framework" (modal verb makes the aspirational nature explicit).

Now a new reader sees the claim AND the honesty caveat together. Aspiration distinguished from current evidence from completed proof.

title: Suggestion: surface Runtime Architecture and Cross-Spec Interactions in the human understanding path
description: The new Runtime-Architecture and Cross-Spec-Interactions docs are exactly the kind of material a technically curious human wants once they understand the basic story. Consider elevating them in the root README or guide as the next stop after the introductory chapters, rather than leaving them buried inside the specification companion set. That would give humans a clearer bridge from narrative understanding to system understanding.
close_reason: Surfaced Runtime-Architecture and Cross-Spec-Interactions in the human path as the system-understanding bridge. Two additions: guide README got a new section After the chapters — the system-understanding bridge with detailed pointers to both docs. Root README For humans subsection added a parallel pointer between examples and the AI/implementor track. RA framed as birds-eye runtime view with ASCII data-flow; CSI framed as 20 edge cases at Spec boundaries. Both written precisely so the AI track uses them, accessibly so the human track can too. Position is between narrative-understanding and system-understanding, exactly where the bead asked.

title: Suggestion: calibrate how strongly the repo presents examples as working code
description: The repo currently recommends the examples as the next step for humans, but some flagship examples are still intentionally partial or scaffolded. Consider making the maturity of each example family more explicit at the repo level so newcomers know which examples are solid trust anchors, which are pedagogical sketches, and which are partial worked scaffolds.
close_reason:
Calibrated example maturity at the repo level.

(1) Created examples/README.md (~50 lines) — explicit Maturity table with three tags:
- Pedagogical sketch (counter, login, routing, ssr) — single-file, one-mechanism focused
- Benchmark (7guis, nine-states) — exhaustive demonstration of a class of UI tasks
- Worked scaffold (realworld) — substantial app, intentionally partial

Per-example table with maturity tag + what it demonstrates. Reading order recommendation. Status reminder that examples are against the imagined v2 API and dont run until the reference implementation lands.

(2) Updated root README §Documentation to point at examples/README.md, list nine-states, mention maturity tagging, and note the running-status caveat.

(3) Updated root README §Reading order > For humans path to use maturity-grouped framing (sketches / benchmarks / worked scaffold) instead of flat list.

A newcomer now sees: (a) examples are organised by maturity; (b) which to read first; (c) that they wont run yet.

title: Suggestion: simplify the human newcomer path so the repo explains itself before it explains the corpus
description: A human reader currently gets several overlapping orientation stories: the root README, the guide README, and the specification README all explain where to start in different ways. Consider tightening this into one clear newcomer path: what re-frame2 is, what kind of project this repo is, what to read first, and what to read second. The benefit is lower onboarding friction for technically interested humans who are not yet ready to navigate the full corpus taxonomy.
close_reason:
Tightened the human newcomer path so each of the three READMEs has one clear job.

Newcomer path now: root README → guide README → guide chapters → examples → spec README (for AIs/implementors).

Trimmed guide README to remove orientation overlap with root README:
- Removed "What re-frame2 is, in one paragraph" (root README has this — AI First? section + tagline + What exists today)
- Removed "A note about the layered docs" (root README Reading order section explains the two-track split)
- Removed "Implementing the pattern in another host" (AI/implementor concern; spec README is the natural home)
- Added a short "if you came straight here, see root README first" note

Kept guide README focused: opening (what this guide IS) → Chapters table → Voice → Whats not here → Pattern docs. That is now the guide-track-only orientation.

Updated spec README Capability layer table to include 013-Flows, and Companion layer table to include the three new companion docs (Runtime-Architecture, Cross-Spec-Interactions, Ownership) that exist now.

Each README now has one job:
- Root: what is re-frame2, what exists today, why look, where to read first
- Guide: human-track contents page, voice statement, what is not here, pattern docs
- Spec: AI/implementor-track corpus index, organisation taxonomy, reading tracks for that audience

title: Suggestion: reconcile repo-level indexes so the spec surface looks current and trustworthy
description: The repo-level entry docs should agree on the current shape of the corpus. Right now there is visible drift around principle count and the numbered-spec range, especially now that Spec 013 exists. Consider making the root README, specification README, and any summary tables share one up-to-date inventory so a newcomer does not have to wonder which document is authoritative.
close_reason:
Reconciled drift across the repo-level indexes:

(1) Principle count: was "11 AI-first practical principles" or "8 AI-first properties" in various places. After P9 (Name over place) was added, the count is 9 discipline principles. Updated:
- 000-Vision.md: 11 → 9 discipline principles
- AI-Audit.md abstract: eight → nine AI-first properties
- Construction-Prompts.md: eight → nine AI-first properties
- Principles.md: "11 principles below" → "9 discipline principles, 2 foundational essays, 1 deliverable principle"
- specification/README.md (2 places): 11 → 9 AI-first discipline principles

(2) Spec range: was "000–012" in several places. Spec 013 (Flows) now exists; updated to "000–013":
- specification/README.md (taxonomy and About-Spec-003 note)
- AI-Audit.md (corpus listing)
- Various per-area Specs in 001–013 references

(3) Root README project layout: was missing 013-Flows.md, Conventions.md, Ownership.md from the directory listing. Added all three.

10 replacements across 6 files; no residual drift on principle count or spec range.

Now the root README, specification README, AI-Audit, Principles, Construction-Prompts, and 000-Vision all agree on the corpus shape.

title: Suggestion: make the root README state clearly what exists today vs what is planned
description: From a newcomer's perspective, the root README currently mixes present-tense claims about a reference implementation and AI tooling with later statements that those pieces are still drafted, partial, or TBD. Consider making the top-level status story explicit and consistent so a human reader can tell, in the first screenful, what is real today, what is scaffolded, and what is planned.
close_reason:
Made the README state today vs planned explicitly in the first screenful.

(1) Updated tagline: "plus a Clojure/CLJS reference implementation, plus AI tooling" → "plus a planned Clojure/CLJS reference implementation, plus planned AI tooling that pairs with apps built on it." Honest about which artefacts exist today.

(2) Added §What exists today — a status table right after the tagline (in the first screenful) covering all 7 components: Specification corpus (drafted; mostly locked), Human-facing guide (drafted), Conformance corpus (43 fixtures), CLJS reference implementation (NOT YET BUILT — explicit), Worked examples (partial), Runtime pair tool (planned), Pair-improver skill (planned). Each with one-sentence honest status.

(3) Closing punchline: "the spec is the deliverable today; the reference implementation, the runtime tooling, and the pair-improver skill are downstream artefacts the spec is designed to produce."

(4) Renamed the bottom Status section to §Status — detail, since the headline is now at top. Kept the layered-completeness breakdown (Locked / Drafted-but-unimplemented / Skeleton) for readers who want the deeper breakdown. Cross-linked.

A newcomer now sees in the first screenful: the project is a spec; some components exist; some are planned. No misleading present-tense claims about non-existent artefacts.

title: Decide disposition of generalised reg and generalised subscribe (re-frame.alpha leftovers)
description:
Follow-up to rf2-tbli, which is closed once flows are locked. Two pieces of re-frame.alpha remain undecided:

## 1. Generalised registration — `(reg :sub ...)` / `(reg :legacy-sub ...)` / `(reg :sub-lifecycle ...)`

A single multi-arity entry point dispatching on a `kind` keyword. Sugar over the existing kind-specific `reg-*` macros plus the `:sub-lifecycle` extension hook.

Four-disposition evaluation per item:
- Drop in favour of per-kind macros (status quo: `reg-event-fx`, `reg-sub`, etc.).
- Promote and extend (`reg` becomes canonical for all 13 v2 registry kinds; per-kind macros stay as sugar).
- Keep alpha, reshape later.
- Reshape and ship (per-kind macros stay; `reg` survives only as the dispatch entry for `:sub-lifecycle`).

Decision needed: which.

## 2. Generalised subscribe — `sub` with query-maps + lifecycle policies

Two changes from `re-frame.core/subscribe`:
- Query-map shape (`{:re-frame/q ::id :param 1 :re-frame/lifecycle :reactive}`) instead of vector.
- Pluggable cache-lifecycle policies: `:safe`, `:no-cache`, `:reactive`, `:forever`, plus user-defined via `(reg :sub-lifecycle ...)`.

Decisions needed:
- Query-map shape: promote (canonical), accept both forms (two ways), drop?
- Lifecycle policies: ship the four built-in policies as the canonical surface? Drop them and have the v2 sub-cache handle lifecycle internally? Make them an opt-in advanced feature?

The lifecycle-policies question is the more substantial — it affects how Spec 006 (Subscription cache) describes disposal. The current 006 §Subscription cache contract specifies a single ref-counting algorithm; lifecycle policies would either layer on top of that or replace it.

## Cross-references

- [013-Flows.md](docs/specification/013-Flows.md) — flows already locked (the third leg of the alpha-namespace tripod).
- [Spec 006 §Subscription cache](docs/specification/006-ReactiveSubstrate.md#subscription-cache--contract-and-operational-semantics) — the lifecycle-policy decision affects this contract.

## Why P2

Alpha-namespace ambiguity is "did you mean this or that" tax. v2 should not ship with alpha-still-alpha.

title: Suggestion: make Cross-Spec Interactions explicit about which rules are pinned vs still awaiting fixtures
description: Cross-Spec Interactions improves completeness, but many entries are written as decided behavior while still pointing to future-fixture placeholders. Consider making the provisional status more explicit, or prioritising fixture coverage for the interactions already written, so readers can tell which cross-spec rules are fully pinned by the conformance corpus and which are still documented-but-unpinned.
close_reason:
Added explicit Pinned vs Provisional status framework to Cross-Spec-Interactions.md. Updated §How to read to introduce the Status field; added a §Status legend with markers and meanings; added a current-state-of-corpus note marking all 20 interactions as Provisional (no fixtures exist yet for any of them — the corpus has zero cross-Spec-interactions fixtures). Replaced all 20 entries Conformance: future fixture X form with Status: Provisional — fixture pending X form. Fixed two entries (4 and 15) that were ambiguous about fixture existence — both are correctly Provisional now.

When fixtures land in future the entries can flip to Pinned individually. The doc now reads honestly about the gap between documented decisions and corpus-enforced behaviour.

title: Suggestion: simplify the specification README front door by splitting index, reading paths, and ownership reference
description: The specification README is doing several useful jobs at once: corpus taxonomy, document index, reading-guide, and ownership matrix. The content is strong, but together it makes the front door heavier than it needs to be. Consider keeping README as a shorter index/front door and moving either the reading-path guidance or the ownership matrix into a dedicated companion doc so new readers can orient faster.
close_reason: Split specification README front door by extracting the Ownership matrix into a dedicated companion doc. Created docs/specification/Ownership.md (44 lines) carrying the full contract-surface → owning-Spec → companion-citations table with the drift rule. README shrank from 171 to 135 lines (~36 lines lighter). The README now contains: How the corpus is organised (taxonomy) → Documents (Foundation/Capability/Companion tables) → Reading paths (Track 1/2/3) → Ownership matrix (now a 3-line pointer to Ownership.md). Added Ownership.md to the README Companion-layer table for discoverability. The reading-paths guidance stayed in README because the Tracks (Canonical/Background/Implementor) read as part of the front-door orientation; only the deep reference (the ownership matrix itself, used to navigate when an implementor is looking for a specific contract surface) moved out. Bead asked for either reading-paths OR ownership matrix to move out — moved the matrix because reading paths are a front-door affordance while the matrix is a deep-reference table.

title: Suggestion: promote live routing semantics from 012 Notes into the main contract
description: Spec 012 still hides some load-bearing routing behavior inside Notes, especially route-id vs URL-string navigation, plain-anchor non-interception, and same-route param-change behavior. These are not incidental notes; they affect both implementors and users. Consider moving them into the normative sections so the front-loaded routing surface is complete without requiring a second pass through the appendix.
close_reason: Promoted three Notes items into 012-Routings normative sections. (1) Route-id vs URL-string navigation form — added as §Target form — route-id vs URL-string subsection inside §Navigation is an event, after the opts-map description. Names route-id as canonical and URL-string as the escape hatch with explicit examples and resolution rule. (2) Linking from views / plain-anchor semantics — promoted to its own §Linking from views section between §URL changes are events and §Reading the route is a sub. Lock statement at top, behaviour table, the why-the-runtime-does-not-auto-intercept rationale. (3) Route reloading: same-route param changes — already covered in §Per-route data loading §3 (line 451), redundant Notes content removed. §Notes section deleted entirely; doc moves directly from §Multi-frame routing to §Open questions.

title: Suggestion: move load-bearing SSR rules out of 011 appendix sections into the main contract
description: Spec 011 still keeps important implementation rules under Resolved decisions. Platform gating, JVM-runnable rendering, routing handshake, fragment behavior, auth/session flow, :after under SSR, and hydration artefact scope are active contract, not historical notes. Consider promoting these rules into the main body and leaving the appendix for genuinely historical or superseded decisions only.
close_reason:
Promoted §Resolved decisions out of historical-appendix framing to active-contract framing in 011-SSR. (1) Section renamed Resolved decisions → Operational rules. (2) Section moved up the doc to BEFORE §Open questions (was after) so it sits in the main-body flow rather than as an appendix. (3) Top-of-section framing note added explicitly stating these are normative rules, active contract not historical notes, and listing what they cover (platform gating, JVM-runnable rendering, routing handshake, fragment behaviour, auth/session flow, :after carve-out for machines, hydration-payload scope). (4) The 8 subsections content is unchanged — they were already well-organised; the issue was purely the framing/positioning. Implementations now encounter these rules in the natural reading flow rather than at the end after Open questions.

Note: did NOT do the more aggressive promotion of merging the 4 items with main-body counterparts (Mismatch recovery into §Hydration-mismatch detection, Effect handling on server into §:platforms metadata, JVM-runnable rendering into §Render-tree → HTML emitter, Hydration of non-state artefacts into §:rf/hydrate event). Those mergers would have eliminated some duplication but at the cost of restructuring 4 main-body subsections and increasing risk of cross-reference drift. Bead is satisfied by the rename + repositioning + framing change without that surgery.

title: Suggestion: trim Spec 005 to the machine contract and move comparative/future material out
description: Spec 005 is still carrying too many roles at once. The core transition-table grammar and machine runtime contract are strong, but the same file also carries open questions, xstate comparison, future work, and disposition material. Now that CP-5-MachineGuide exists, consider keeping 005 focused on the normative machine surface and moving explanatory/comparative material out so the main spec is easier to read linearly.
close_reason:
Trimmed Spec 005 by moving comparative/explanatory material to CP-5-MachineGuide.md.

Moved:
1. §Substitutes for skipped features (parallel regions → separate machines, history states → snapshot capture, full worked examples, why-xstate-needs-history-but-re-frame2-doesnt rationale) — ~127 lines.
2. §Lessons from xstate (deliberate divergences) — table of 8 design choices with rationale, plus convergences statement — ~20 lines.

005 line count: 1960 → 1825 (135 lines lighter). CP-5-MachineGuide line count: 85 → 222.

In 005 each moved section is now a 2-3 sentence pointer to the CP-5 location. The pointers preserve enough context that a 005 reader knows what was moved out and where to find it; the meat lives in CP-5 where it belongs (CP-5 is the explanatory/companion doc; 005 is the normative contract).

Kept in 005 (per the bead these are not the comparative material to move out):
- §Open questions — spec-tracking metadata
- §Resolved decisions — spec-tracking metadata
- §Future — post-v1 design sketches
