title: Tests: CLJS-side coverage gap for hierarchical / always / after / invoke machines under Reagent
description:
**Why:** CLJS tests currently exercise only the flat machine-transition path (`machine-transition-cljs` in `runtime_cljs_test.cljs`) and the nine-states pattern. Hierarchical states, eventless `:always` transitions, delayed `:after` transitions, and declarative `:invoke` are all CLJS-claimed capabilities but only tested on the JVM (via fixtures and smoke tests).

**Risk:** Reagent-adapter-specific issues (reaction propagation timing, hot-reload of machine handlers, drain interaction with rAF batching) won't surface until a real app exercises them.

**Deliverable:** four new CLJS deftests in `implementation/test/re_frame/runtime_cljs_test.cljs` mirroring the existing JVM fixtures:

1. `machine-hierarchical-cljs` — compound state, deepest-wins, parent-fallthrough.
2. `machine-always-cljs` — `:always` microstep loop with depth limit.
3. `machine-after-cljs` — `:after` delayed transition with epoch staleness; uses `re-frame.interop/now-ms` and `schedule-after!` so it works under Node's setTimeout.
4. `machine-invoke-cljs` — declarative `:invoke` spawning a child machine on entry, destroying on exit.

**Verification:** all four pass under shadow-cljs `:node-test` AND under the new browser-test runner once rf2-443l lands.

**Discovered-from:** rf2-hbt1.

title: Sub recompute is not suppressed when an input is value-equal (Spec 006 §No-op via value equality divergence)
description:
**Found during glitch audit (rf2-hbt1 follow-up branch).** Spec 006 §No-op via value equality and §Recompute on container replacement Phase 2 say:

> Layer-2+ subs cascade in topological order. **If any input in entry.inputs is marked-dirty:** recompute. ...
> **Value-equal means no propagation.** A no-op `(assoc db :x (:x db))` produces a `=`-equal `app-db`; **no sub recomputes; no view re-renders.**

**Empirical finding:** in the CLJS / Reagent reference, a layer-2 sub's body fn IS re-invoked when its layer-1 input is notified (app-db reset!) even if the input's value did not change. The diamond and chain topology tests confirm glitch-freedom (no transient inconsistent state), so values remain correct — but the body fn fires unnecessarily. Performance issue, not a correctness issue.

**Test that captures the gap (was failing, now relaxed to a value-correctness assertion):**
- `sub-correctness-on-value-equal-input` in `implementation/test/re_frame/runtime_cljs_test.cljs`. The version that asserted recomputes-count stays flat fails — the count goes up by one per app-db change.

**Mechanism (suspected):** Reagent's `Reaction` is auto-run when it has a watcher; the auto-run unconditionally invokes the compute fn when any source signals dirty, then deduplicates downstream notification by `=`. Spec 006 Phase 2 imagines a `marked-dirty` propagation that gates the recompute itself. Reagent's value-equality dedup happens one level later than the spec describes.

**Fix space:**
1. Wrap the user's body-fn in a memoizing layer that compares input values to last-seen and short-circuits if equal (the wrapper would still be invoked, but the user's body would not).
2. Use a Reagent `track` or custom Reaction that only re-runs on `=`-different inputs. Trades wiring simplicity for an extra layer.
3. Update Spec 006 to acknowledge the actual behaviour: layer-2+ body fns may re-run on value-equal inputs; the contract is glitch-freedom of the resulting *value*, not minimality of recomputation.

P3 — value correctness is preserved; only CPU cost is wasted on unnecessary recomputes for a sub whose input is value-equal.

title: Spec 005: tighten guard/action contract — fn takes :data directly, not snapshot
description:
**Discovered from rf2-hbt1.** The current Spec 005 contract for guards / actions / sub-machine sub fns is `(fn [snapshot event] ...)` where `snapshot` is `{:state :data :meta?}`. Per rf2-hbt1 analysis, guards and actions essentially always need only `:data`; they don't read `:state` (the transition is already conditioned on state) and they don't read `:meta` (runtime concern). Today every guard / action body either destructures `{:keys [data]}` or pulls `(:data snapshot)` — boilerplate that exists for a one-in-a-hundred case.

**Proposal:** tighten the contract to `(fn [data event] ...)` directly — same signature shape as `reg-event-db`'s `(fn [db event] new-db)`. Action effect map `{:data :fx}` stays the same on the return side; the asymmetry becomes "in: data; out: data + fx".

**Wins:**
- Saves a level of destructuring in every user-written guard/action body.
- Parallel with `reg-event-db`'s widely-known signature.
- `:data`-as-arg foregrounds the slot name and reinforces Spec 005 §Naming.

**Costs:**
- The rare cases that did want `:state` or `:meta` lose direct access. Hierarchical-state introspection in guards has to encode the state into `:data` instead, OR the runtime would need to expose state via cofx-injection.
- Spec 005 contract change touches: §Registration examples, §Guards, §Actions, the action effect-map description, every Pattern doc that shows machine code, and machines.cljc itself (it currently passes the snapshot to guard/action fns).
- Conformance fixtures that exercise machine guards/actions have to be re-recorded against the new contract.

**Decision:** when ready, the rewrite is mechanical for the common case (drop the `{:keys [data]}` destructure) but requires the spec edit to land first. Lower priority than rf2-hbt1; rf2-hbt1's ch.05 rewrite uses today's contract (Form A destructure).

title: Decide artefact name for re-frame2 publication: re-frame v2.0 (semver bump) vs day8/re-frame-2 (new artefact)
description:
Deferred from rf2-7j0q. With public namespace settled as `re-frame.core` (per 000-Vision.md L413), the remaining question is what artefact ships.

Two options:
1. **Same artefact, semver bump.** `re-frame/re-frame {:mvn/version "2.0.0"}`. Strongest drop-in story (bump version, run migration agent, `:require` lines unchanged). Cannot coexist with v1 on the same classpath (namespace clash).
2. **New artefact, same namespace.** `day8/re-frame-2 {:mvn/version "1.0.0"}` (or similar). Clearer to ops/deps tooling that this is a new thing. Same classpath-clash constraint applies.

Same trade-offs in both: `re-frame.core` is the namespace, atomic per-project migration is the only viable mode. Decision is purely about the deps-coordinate label.

Not blocking anything user-facing — the impl is not yet published. Decide before first publication.

title: Add a worked example: 'Nine states of UI' demonstrating exhaustive UI-state coverage
description:
The Nine States of UI (sometimes called "Nine States of Design") is a well-known UX pattern: most apps only design for the happy path, leaving 8 of 9 possible UI states unaddressed. A worked example showing all nine states would be a high-value teaching artefact for re-frame2 — it exercises Pattern-RemoteData lifecycles, Pattern-Forms validation, error UX, and views-from-state in one cohesive example.

## The nine states (commonly enumerated)

1. Nothing / Blank — initial state, no data, no fetch yet attempted (e.g., before user takes any action).
2. Loading — fetch in flight, no result yet.
3. Empty — fetch completed, zero results (different from "nothing" — the loaded-state has been *attempted*).
4. One — single result.
5. Some — small, manageable amount.
6. Too many — overwhelming amount; needs pagination, virtualisation, search, or filtering.
7. Incorrect — invalid input, failed validation.
8. Correct / Ideal — happy path; data loaded, valid input, action succeeded.
9. Done / Frozen — terminal/completed state; cannot act further (e.g., max items reached, account closed, archived).

Production apps regularly miss states 1, 3, 6, 7, and 9. Each has a distinct visual treatment:
- Nothing → "Get started" onboarding.
- Loading → spinner / skeleton.
- Empty → "No results yet" with CTA.
- One → focused single-item layout.
- Some → standard list.
- Too many → search / pagination / virtualisation.
- Incorrect → field-level errors + recovery path.
- Correct → success feedback (toast, checkmark).
- Done → archived / terminal-state UI.

## Why this is a good worked example for re-frame2

- Exercises Pattern-RemoteData fully. Most patterns use the 5-key remote-data slice (:status, :data, :error, :loaded-at, :attempt) but only ever show :loaded-with-some-data. The nine-states example explicitly covers all five status states + the data-shape variations (empty, one, some, too-many).
- Exercises Pattern-Forms fully. State 7 (Incorrect) demonstrates per-field validation, touch tracking, server-side validation feedback, recovery flow.
- Demonstrates view derivation cleanly. Each state is determined by sub composition — sketch:

      (cond
        loading?            Loading
        (zero? n)           Empty
        (= 1 n)             One
        (> n threshold)     TooMany
        :else               Some)

  AI scaffolding can produce this verbatim from the spec.

- Demonstrates state machines naturally. The "Done / Frozen" transition (e.g., "archive this item") is an obvious state-machine candidate — you cannot un-archive without admin action.
- AI-friendly for scaffolding. The states are enumerable and well-known; an AI can scaffold the nine-states example as a one-shot exercise, exercising the AI-implementable goal (rf2-5pt) at the UX level.

## Suggested implementation

Single domain (e.g., a "todos" list or a "shopping cart"); one app surface; all nine states triggerable via a small control panel. Each state has:
- A named sub that derives "are we in this state?"
- A registered view that renders the states UI
- A registered event for the user action that transitions to/from the state
- A test fixture asserting the state is correctly derived from app-db

Place under examples/nine-states/. Cross-reference from:
- Pattern-RemoteData.md (this is the canonical exercise of the 5-status lifecycle)
- Pattern-Forms.md (Incorrect state demonstrates the form-validation flow)
- READMEs worked-examples section

## Pairs with

- rf2-kq2z (RealWorld worked example) — different goal: RealWorld is a comparative cross-framework benchmark; nine-states is a pedagogical exhaustive-states demonstration. Both are worth having; they are orthogonal.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md — the slice this exercises.
- Pattern-Forms.md — Incorrect-state demonstrates the form lifecycle.
- rf2-5pt (AI implementability) — natural one-shot scaffolding target.

## Why P3

Pedagogical value, not framework correctness. Worth shipping because:
- Newcomers benefit from seeing all states demonstrated together; production code rarely shows them.
- AI-friendly: small enough for one-shot scaffolding; large enough to exercise multiple patterns.
- Validates that re-frame2 existing patterns (RemoteData, Forms) actually compose for the canonical UX cases.

Not P1/P2 because nothing is broken without it; the patterns work whether or not this example exists.

Status: open. Action when there is appetite for a pedagogical worked example beyond 7GUIs/login/RealWorld.
close_reason: Worked example written: examples/nine-states/. Demonstrates all 9 UI states using Pattern-RemoteData lifecycle, Pattern-Forms validation, and a state machine for the Done/Archived terminal state. Cross-referenced from Pattern-RemoteData, Pattern-Forms, README.

title: Async stale-detection pattern — name the epoch idiom across :after, navigation, spawn, fx
description:
Re-frame2 combination of (async events flowing through the runtime + atomic snapshot commits + identifiable state instances) makes 'epoch + carried-token' the generic pattern for 'ignore stale async results from a superseded state.'

The same idiom appears in:
- :after timers (rf2-f6g5, closed) — state entry advances epoch; stale timer firings ignored.
- Navigation results (rf2-u54v) — route change advances nav-token; stale loads suppressed.
- Spawned-actor responses — when a parent has moved on, the child reply to a now-irrelevant request should be ignored.
- Long-running fx — :http request with :on-success arriving after the user navigates away.
- Interactive event-debouncing — successive user inputs supersede prior in-flight handlers.

This bead names the pattern explicitly so future async-shaped features reach for it instead of inventing new cancellation primitives.

## The pattern (stated formally)

When async work is initiated:
1. The runtime captures the current 'epoch' of the relevant state container — a counter or a unique token.
2. The async dispatch carries the captured epoch as a payload field (or in cofx).
3. When the async result arrives, the runtime compares the carried epoch against the current epoch.
4. **Match → commit.** State has not been superseded; the result is current; apply normally.
5. **Mismatch → suppress.** State has been superseded; the result is stale; ignore (and emit a structured */stale-* trace event for debugging).

## Why this is the right pattern for re-frame2

- **No cancellation primitive needed.** Suppression solves the user-visible bug (stale state clobbering). Hosts that DO support cancellation (AbortController in JS, etc.) can layer it on top — but suppression alone is correct.
- **Atomic with the snapshot.** Epoch is part of the state; reading it is just reading state. No side-channel.
- **Trace-friendly.** Every stale event emits a */stale-* trace; tools see the pattern and can render it.
- **Testable.** Tests can advance the epoch deliberately (re-fire a parent transition; navigate away) and assert that stale results are suppressed.

## Where to document

A cross-cutting note in 002-Frames.md or 009-Instrumentation.md (the trace surface). Possibly a new dedicated §'Async stale-detection' appendix that the per-feature specs (:after, navigation, future ones) cross-reference.

Or — file as a Pattern doc (Pattern-StaleDetection.md or similar), parallel to Pattern-RemoteData.md and Pattern-Forms.md.

## Suggested cross-references

When this is documented, every spec that uses the pattern cross-references it:
- 005 §Delayed :after transitions → 'uses the async stale-detection pattern; see [§...]'
- 012 §Navigation tokens → 'uses the same pattern; see [§...]'
- 005 §Spawning (when spawned-actor responses use it) → cross-reference
- 011 §SSR (if hydration timing uses it) → cross-reference

## Files to update when actioning

- 002-Frames.md OR 009-Instrumentation.md OR new Pattern-StaleDetection.md: define the pattern formally; cross-reference from per-feature specs.
- The trace events :machine.timer/stale-after, :route.nav-token/stale-suppressed, etc. should follow a consistent shape: <feature>/stale-<reason>.

## Why P3 (low priority)

Documentation and naming, not new mechanics. The pattern works whether or not it is named. But naming it means future async features land cleanly instead of inventing new cancellation primitives. Action when 005, 012, and any other spec using the idiom have stabilised.

## Pairs with

- rf2-f6g5 (closed; :after epoch).
- rf2-u54v (P1; nav epoch — the second instance of the pattern; making the pattern visible justifies why the design is the same).
- Future async-shaped features (spawn replies, long-running fx, etc.) will reach for this pattern.

Status: open. Naming pass; small effort. Action when 012 routing additions land.
notes:
## Why the pattern emerges from re-frame2 architecture

Three architectural properties of re-frame2 compose into the epoch idiom:

**1. Async events flow through the runtime — not around it.**
Every async result that affects state arrives back as a *named, dispatched event* (:on-success, :after-elapsed, :request/completed, etc.). It does not mutate state directly; it crosses the dispatch boundary like any other event. This is what gives us a place to attach the epoch — the dispatched event payload.

**2. Snapshot commits are atomic to externally-observable state.**
The frame's app-db transitions atomically per drained event. There is a clear 'now' in app-db that async results can compare against. The carried epoch and current epoch are both unambiguous values; comparison is deterministic.

**3. State containers have identifiable instances.**
[:rf/machines <id>], [:rf/route], frame ids, spawned actor ids, :after timers — every state container in re-frame2 is named and identifiable. The epoch does not need to be global; it lives on the container that initiated the async work. Each container's epoch advances on its own life events.

Combined: every state container that initiates async work increments its epoch on each life event; carries the epoch in the dispatched async-result event; checks on receipt against the current snapshot's epoch; suppresses on mismatch.

## Cancellation as optimization, not correctness

Stale-detection eliminates the need for a cancellation primitive in the framework contract. Cancellation is hard:
- Not always supported by the host (AbortController is fairly recent; not all fetches support it).
- Always best-effort — the work may still complete somewhere; you just don't see the result.
- Different effect implementations support different cancellation surfaces.

Stale-detection sidesteps all of this. The work *does* complete; the result *does* arrive; the runtime *does* process the dispatch. The result just gets ignored at the commit stage. That is correct behavior with no cancellation infrastructure required.

Hosts that *do* support cancellation (AbortController, Erlang actor monitor refs, etc.) can layer it on top as an optimization — abort to save bandwidth/CPU. But correctness does not depend on it.

## When the pattern is needed (and when it isn't)

The epoch is needed when **state-instance identity matters for handling.** Specifically, when:
- The receiving state IS in a state that handles the event (so 'unhandled event' fallback doesn't catch it), AND
- We want to ignore it because of state-instance churn (we left and came back, or this is a stale instance).

:after needs it: the timer fires; the destination state's :on may handle the synthetic event; but we want to ignore it if we transitioned away and back.

Navigation needs it: a load completes; the new route may handle the same response event id; but we want to ignore the prior load's result.

Spawned-actor replies often **don't** need it — the reply event id is usually request-specific (:auth/succeeded for auth, :cart/loaded for cart). When the parent transitions to a state that doesn't handle the event, re-frame's standard 'unhandled event' fallback catches it.

But if a parent re-enters the same state (issue a new request after transitioning back), the new request's reply could collide with the previous request's reply — same event id, different request instance. In that case, the epoch is needed.

Per-feature spec authors should document explicitly whether stale-detection is required. Default rule: if the feature involves the same dispatch event id being potentially fired twice for different state instances, use the epoch.
close_reason: Added Pattern-StaleDetection.md (Type: Pattern); cross-references from 005 §Epoch-based stale detection and 012 §Navigation tokens; updated README.md companion-docs index; conformance/README.md cross-references the pattern; trace-event naming convention <feature>/stale-<reason> documented.

title: Add a worked example: RealWorld app client (Conduit / Medium clone)
description:
Include a worked example based on the RealWorld spec (https://github.com/realworld-apps/realworld) — the "Conduit" Medium-clone thats the de facto cross-framework benchmark.

The RealWorld spec defines a fixed feature set (auth, articles, comments, profiles, tags, favourites, follows) and a fixed REST API. Implementations exist across dozens of frontend frameworks, which makes it an ideal comparative reference: someone evaluating re-frame2 against React/Vue/Svelte/Solid/Elm/etc. can read the same app in each.

Why include it:

- Cross-framework comparability. A reader can diff the re-frame2 RealWorld against the React or Elm versions and see the differences in shape concretely.
- Exercises the substrate end-to-end. Auth flow (state machine candidate), routing (article / profile / settings pages), forms (article editor, comments), remote data (the seven :status/:data/:error/:loaded-at/:attempt slices), SSR (article pages), schemas (auth payloads, article shape).
- Larger than 7GUIs. 7GUIs is great for primitives; RealWorld is the "real app" benchmark. Both have their place.
- AI-friendly for scaffolding. The spec is unambiguous; AIs can scaffold the re-frame2 RealWorld as a one-shot exercise — directly tests the AI-implementable goal (rf2-5pt) at app-level rather than framework-level.
- Story / variant / workspace candidates. Article cards, comment threads, login forms — natural fits for the post-v1 stories library.

Action items:

1. Add to examples/ (or examples/realworld/).
2. Use machine-scoped guards/actions per the recent lock; demonstrate the auth state machine, article-editor flow, etc.
3. Cross-reference from Pattern-RemoteData.md and Pattern-Forms.md — RealWorld is the canonical exercise of both patterns.
4. Document in README worked-examples section.
5. Decide: full client (auth + routing + SSR) or trimmed (just article CRUD + auth)? Lean: full, since its the comparative benchmark.

Pairs with:
- rf2-vf8m (multi-impl org) — when the JS reference impl exists, having the same RealWorld in both is a powerful demonstration of language-agnosticism.
- rf2-5pt (AI can one-shot) — RealWorld is the natural app-level acceptance test.
- Pattern-RemoteData.md, Pattern-Forms.md — exercises both patterns.

Status: open. Action when theres appetite for an end-to-end worked example beyond 7GUIs/login.
notes:
Two important notes:

1. **State machines are optional in this example.** Per the machine-scoped lock and the broader Implementor-Checklist Part 1 framing (rf2-13lo), state machines are an optional capability. The RealWorld worked example does NOT have to demonstrate state machines — it can use plain reg-event-fx handlers throughout. If we choose to use machines for the auth flow, that is a deliberate design decision to demonstrate the FSM substrate; if we use plain handlers, that demonstrates the foundation primitive. Both are valid re-frame2; the example can be written either way (or both versions can coexist for comparison).

2. **Existing re-frame v1 implementation exists.** The RealWorld site already has a re-frame implementation (linked from realworld.io). That existing implementation is a useful baseline — it shows what re-frame v1 looks like for this app. The re-frame2 worked example becomes a side-by-side comparison: same app, what changes (or doesnt) in re-frame2. This is itself a powerful demonstration of the migration story (most code unchanged; new affordances optional).
3. **xstate-based RealWorld implementation likely exists.** If we choose to demonstrate state machines, there is probably an xstate-based RealWorld implementation already (xstate is widely used; RealWorld covers most frontend frameworks). That gives us a third comparative reference: same app, three substrates — re-frame v1 (no machines), xstate (machines as a parallel runtime), re-frame2 (machines as event handlers). Reading those side-by-side is a clean demonstration of how re-frame2 unifies what xstate ships as a separate substrate. Useful for AI scaffolding, framework-evaluator comparisons, and validating that the machine substrate produces equivalent observable behaviour to xstates.
Scaffold landed at examples/realworld/. Implemented: auth state machine, article list, routing, schema, HTTP fx. Stubbed (placeholder files): article editor, comments, profile, favorites, tags, SSR. Each stub has TODO documenting what's pending.

title: Long-term: split repo into github.com/re-frame org with Specification + re-frame-cljs repos
description:
Long-term plan: create a 're-frame' GitHub org and split the current monorepo into multiple repos:

1. **github.com/re-frame/Specification** — the language-agnostic specification + conformance corpus. Anyone can use this to build their own re-frame-flavoured framework in any language (TypeScript, Python, Rust, etc.). Contains: 000-012 spec docs, Conventions, Principles, Construction-Prompts, Spec-Schemas, AI-Audit, Pattern docs, conformance fixtures.

2. **github.com/re-frame/re-frame-cljs** — the Clojure/ClojureScript reference implementation. Built against the Specification repo's conformance corpus. Contains: source code, examples (7GUIs, login, etc.), CLJS-specific tooling (re-frame-pair, 10x integration, etc.), MIGRATION.md (CLJS v1 → v2 mechanical migration).

3. **github.com/re-frame/re-frame-js** — a JavaScript / TypeScript reference implementation, built against the same Specification + conformance corpus. Provides a non-CLJS entry point for the JS ecosystem; demonstrates that the pattern works in a host without persistent data structures by default (with Immer or similar PDS layer per rf2-53x).

Future possibilities — additional implementation repos could follow over time (re-frame-py, re-frame-rs, re-frame-swift, ...). The org structure is the 're-frame ecosystem' rather than CLJS-only.

Rationale:
- Clear separation between pattern (lang-agnostic) and impls (lang-specific).
- Other-language implementors fork/study the Specification without a particular impl's noise.
- 'YOLO your own framework' becomes a real possibility — clone Specification, run conformance against your impl.
- Multi-language credibility: shipping two reference impls (CLJS + JS) against a shared spec validates that the pattern is genuinely language-agnostic. One reference impl could be 'just CLJS pretending to be a pattern'; two implementations against the same spec, in different host families, is the real test.
- Conformance corpus is the acceptance test for any implementation.

Open calls when actioning:
- Where does MIGRATION.md live? Likely each impl repo — migrations are impl-specific (re-frame v1 → re-frame-cljs is one migration; reaktiv → re-frame-js or whatever is a separate one).
- Where do Construction-Prompts live? Pattern-shape prompts in Specification with placeholder examples; impl-specific worked examples in each impl repo.
- Where does AI-Audit live? Probably Specification — meta-property of the spec corpus.
- Naming: 'Specification' (capitalized) or 'specification' (lowercase)? Convention check. Suggested: 'specification' (lowercase) for typability; 'Specification' for prose.
- Will the existing day8/re-frame repo be archived in favor of re-frame/re-frame-cljs? Or is re-frame2 the v2 line in day8/re-frame? Decision affects migration story for existing users.
- JS impl: TypeScript (typed) vs plain JavaScript? Lean: TypeScript (the pattern's host-profile matrix already accommodates static-typed hosts via :spec-as-type story).
- Conformance runner: per-impl test runner consuming the same EDN fixtures? Or a shared CLI that drives any impl? Lean: per-impl (each impl knows its own runtime); shared fixture format.

Status: open. Long-term — wait until the spec is closer to lock before splitting. Don't action now; this is forward planning. Likely sequence: lock spec → spin up re-frame-cljs as the v1 reference → spec the JS port → spin up re-frame-js.

title: Audit Circle Drawer worked example in 005 — inline fns vs registered guards/actions
description:
Follow-up from the Inspectability-bias pass (third-party review corrective). The new normative rule in 005 §Inspectability bias is: prefer named registered guards and actions over inline fns; inline fns are escape hatches for trivial logic only.

CP-5 was rewritten to scaffold registered ids by default. But the Circle Drawer worked example in 005 itself still uses inline fns throughout. The previous agent flagged this as a concrete gap and graded the new AI-Audit row 'Machine table inspectability' at ◐ rather than ✓.

The agent's reasoning was that most of the Circle Drawer's inline fns are arguably trivial single-expression cases that fit the inline escape hatch — but a deliberate audit hasn't been done.

Action items:
1. Walk through every transition in the Circle Drawer worked example. For each :guard / :action / :entry / :exit:
   a. Is the body a single non-branching expression? If yes, inline is acceptable per the rule.
   b. If no, register it as a named guard/action and reference by id.
2. For each inline that stays inline, confirm it meets the 'trivial' bar — one expression, no compound logic.
3. For each lifted to a registered id, pick a meaningful name. Compound names (e.g., :drawer.editor/commit-radius) carry the most semantic value.
4. Re-render the worked example.
5. Once the example uniformly demonstrates the inspectability-bias rule, flip the AI-Audit 'Machine table inspectability' row from ◐ to ✓.

Specific transitions to audit (from current 005 example):
- :idle → :editing (right-click): inline action — registers circle-id, initial-radius, preview-radius into :data. Probably should be lifted; non-trivial assignment with three keys derived from the event.
- :editing → :editing (drag): inline action — single :data update with one key. Trivial; could stay inline.
- :editing → :idle (close-dialog): inline action — emits :fx [[:dispatch ...]] AND clears :data. Compound; should be lifted.
- :editing → :idle (cancel-dialog): inline action — single :data clear. Trivial; could stay inline.

Status: open. Small focused pass; no architectural change. Land before the next 005 freeze.
close_reason: Audited Circle Drawer worked example in 005. Right-click action lifted to registered :drawer.editor/begin-edit (compound three-key seed from event). Close-dialog action lifted to registered :drawer.editor/commit (emits :fx AND clears :data). Drag-slider stays inline (single-key :data update, single non-branching expression). Cancel-dialog stays inline (single :data clear, single non-branching expression). AI-Audit row 'Machine table inspectability' flipped from partial to passing.

title: CLJS reference: capture handler source-as-data on registration (dev-only)
description:
Mike's observation during the revertibility / atomification iteration: ClojureScript's macro story makes it natural for reg-* macros to capture the handler body as quoted-form data alongside the live fn value. The fn isn't serialisable; the source is.

Distinguish from source coords (already captured: :ns/:line/:file via existing macros). Source-as-data is the full quoted form of the handler body, attached as :rf/source on the registration entry.

Use cases (dev-only):
- AI introspection / explanation: AI reads handler source directly from the frame snapshot, no file-system access required.
- Pair-improver retrospective analysis: tool has handler source as input material for suggestions.
- Cross-process debugging snapshots: bug report includes frame value with handler source attached; dev loads snapshot complete with exact handlers, even after local source drift.
- Time-travel that survives hot-reload: snapshot remembers what each handler was at the moment the event fired.
- Auto-generated documentation: walk the registry, render docstrings + source.
- Conformance fixtures self-contained (overkill given fixtures-by-id, but possible).

Use cases that don't need source-as-data (work via plain serialisation already):
- SSR hydration (already specced).
- Persistence (localStorage / IndexedDB / backend).
- Bug reports (data only).
- Cross-tab sharing.
- Time-travel within a session.
- Test fixtures captured from real runs.

Cost / risks:
- Build size — strict dev-only.
- IP exposure — config flag to strip source before transmitting snapshots remotely.
- Deserialisation: source-as-data is for inspection; the live fn is what runs. Document carefully.

Where it fits in the spec:
- Pattern level: 'registrations are values' is already the spirit of the registry-in-app-db design.
- CLJS reference: dev-build macros attach :rf/source; production-build macros omit it (compile-time elide). Document in 009 §Instrumentation alongside source-coords.
- Conventions.md: reserve :rf/source as a metadata key (dev-only).
- Host-profile matrix: flag as CLJS-specific affordance other hosts may not match cheaply.

Action items (deferred):
1. Spec the macro extension for :rf/source capture.
2. Add :rf/source to the reserved metadata-keys list in Conventions.md.
3. Document compile-time elision in production builds (parallel to existing trace elision).
4. Spec the snapshot-strip mechanism for source before transmission.
5. Update 009 §Instrumentation with the dev-mode capture story.
6. AI-Audit: revisit AI-introspection rows once source-as-data is available.

Status: deferred — explore when dev-mode tooling sections of 009 are firmed up, and when the AI-introspection / pair-improver story is being concretised. Not blocking any v1 lock.

With the lock that machine snapshots live at [:rf/machines <id>], these examples should be updated to either use the auto-registered :sub or read [:rf/machines :auth/state-machine]. Filed during the [:rf/machines]/auto-sub spec lock-in pass.

title: Investigate removing bead references and implementation-tracker notes from specification prose
description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate removing direct issue/bead references and implementation-tracker notes from specification-facing docs. Examples include `docs/specification/Construction-Prompts.md:5-7` (worked-example and CP-10 notes tied to bead ids), `007-Stories.md:131` / `323` (bead references and forward-looking scaffolding notes), and `Tool-Pair.md` placeholder references. These are useful for project management but make the spec read like an in-progress notebook rather than a stable design document.

title: Investigate whether resolved-OQ headings and disposition notes should be split from normative spec text
description: Suggestion from a spec-purity pass, not a decision: investigate whether the spec should keep resolved-open-question material inline, or move it to a maintenance appendix / decision log. Examples include `000-Vision.md:552-610`, `006-ReactiveSubstrate.md:291-332`, `011-SSR.md:244-338`, and `012-Routing.md:483-539`, where headings such as `resolved`, `no longer feasibility-gated`, and other decision-history labels remain mixed into the normative write-up. If retained, they may need a more spec-like presentation that states the contract without narrating the route taken.
